question: Among the four works mentioned below which one is encyclopedic in nature? (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Amarakosa
option_2: Siddhantasiromani
option_3: Brihat Samhita
option_4: Ashtangahrdaya
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Brihat Samhita is an encyclopedic Hindu text written by Varahamihira in the late 5th or early 6th century CE.
  • The text is written in verse and contains more than 100 chapters. It covers topics such as astronomy, divination, and various arts like architecture, agriculture, and jewelry.
  • The text is preserved in Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist temples and monasteries and exists in many Indian scripts. The Arabian astronomer Albiruni praised the text for its richness in details.

Amarakosha :

  • It is a Sanskrit dictionary written by the ancient Indian scholar Amarasimha in the 4th century AD. It is also known by its popular name, Namalinganushasanam, which means “instruction concerning nouns and gender”.
  • Amarakosha is one of the oldest extant Sanskrit lexicons. It contains 3 kandas with 26 vargas and over 3000 lines. It collects words with similar meanings, along with providing information about their linga.

siddhanta shiromani (Sanskrit for “Crown of treatises”) :

  • It is a major treatise by the Indian mathematician Bhāskara II. It was written in 1150 when Bhāskara II was 36 years old. The book covers arithmetic, algebra, spheres, and the mathematics of planets. T he book is also known as Head Jewel of Accuracy.

Ashtanga Hridaya :

  • It is an ancient Indian Ayurvedic text written by Vagbhata in medieval Kerala. T he title translates to “the essence of eight sections” that Ayurveda is divided into. It’s considered one of the finest texts on Ayurveda and is a primary root text of the subject.
  • Ashtanga Hridaya is a Prakaran Grantha, which means it’s written by quoting references from other texts. It’s neither too abridged nor too elaborated.
  • Ashtanga Hridaya is a basic textbook that every Ayurvedic student should read in college.
  • It continues to serve as a root source for Ayurvedic philosophy and protocol, providing clear guidelines in all aspects of health.

question: Which one of the following is the most fundamental difference between Mahayana Buddhism and Hinyana Buddhism? (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Emphasis on ahimsa
option_2: Casteless society
option_3: Worship of Gods and Goddesses
option_4: Worship of Stupa
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The most fundamental difference between Mahayana Buddhism and Hinayana Buddhism is the worship of gods and goddesses.
  • Mahayana Buddhists worship bodhisattvas and believe in the divinity of Buddha. Hinayana Buddhists believe that Buddha was a human who found the path to Nirvana.
  • Hinayana teachings are more self-serving, and they believe in attaining individual salvation through meditation and self discipline.

Mahayana and Hinayana share some similarities, including:

1. Both follow the same Vinaya

2. Both prohibit the five offenses

3. Both practice the Four Noble Truths

Mahayana Buddhism also differs from older forms of Buddhism in two ways:

1. The creation of new statues of Buddha

2. The idea of Bodhisattvas, who haveattained enlightenment and help others do the same.

question: The earlist evidence of silver in india is found in the......... (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Harappan Culture
option_2: Chalcolithic cultures of Western India
option_3: Vedic tests
option_4: Silver punch marked coins
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The earliest evidence of silver in India is found in the Harappan culture. The Harappan civilization, also known as the Indus Valley Civilization, existed from approximately 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE.
  •  The Harappan culture is known for its advanced metallurgical techniques. Archaeologists have discovered evidence of the use of copper, bronze, tin, silver, and gold. Silver was first extracted and used in the Indus valley civilization, mainly for ornaments such as hair beads and necklaces.
  • The civilization was rediscovered in the 1920s after excavations at Mohenjo-daro in Sindh and Harappa in west Punjab. Among the frequently found metal items of this civilization are silverware, jewelry, gold- and silver-plated beads, and figurines of both humans and animals.

Punch-marked coins :

  • These are the first documented coins in India, dating from the 7th-6th century BC to the 1st century AD. They are also known as Aahat coins.
  • Punch-marked coins are mostly made of silver and feature symbols punched onto the coin with a separate punch. The coins have no inscriptions, but are punched with various symbols, such as human figures, floral and geometrical patterns, religious symbols, trees, and plant motifs.
  • The coins are broadly classified into two periods, the first period attributed to the Janapadas or small local states. They are found over most parts of the subcontinentand remained in circulation till the early centuries CE.

question: In ancient Indian historical geography the name “Ratnakara”denoted......... (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: The Arabian Sea
option_2: The Bay of Bengal
option_3: The Indian ocean
option_4: The confluence of the Ganga, the Yamuna and mythical Saraswati at Prayaga
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • In ancient Indian historical geography, the name “Ratnakara” denoted the Indian Ocean. The name “Ratnakara” translates to “the maker (creator) of gems” in ancient Sanskrit literature.

question: Which one of the following usages was a post - vedic devlopment? (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Dharma - Artha - Kama - Moksha
option_2: Brahama - Kasatriya - Vaishya - Shudra
option_3: Brahmacharya - Grihastashrama - Vanaprastha - Sanyasa
option_4: Indra - Surya - Rudra - Marut
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The correct answer is option 3, Brahmacharya—Grihastashrama— Vanaprastha—Sanyasa. These are the four stages of life in Hindu philosophy that developed during the post-Vedic period.
  • In this system, every person goes through these four stages at appropriate times in life.
  • Sanyasa is a form of asceticism that involves renouncing material desires and prejudices.
  • It is traditionally conceptualized for men or women in late years of their life. However, young brahmacharis can choose to skip the householder and retirement stages to dedicate their lives to spiritual pursuits.

question: The name of the poet kalidasa is mentioned in the......... (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Allahabad pillar inscription
option_2: Aihole inscription
option_3: Alapadu grant
option_4: Hanamkonda inscription
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The name of the poet Kalidasa is mentioned in the Aihole inscription. The inscription is located at Aihole in Karnataka state. It was written by Ravikirti, the court poet of Chalukya King, Pulakeshi II, who reigned from 610 to 642 CE. The inscription is in Sanskrit and Kannada, and celebrates the king’s victory, as well as mentioning other great poets.
  • The Aihole inscription is also known as the Aihole prashasti or Aihole abhilekh. It mentions the famous poet and dramatist Kalidasa (4-5th century C.E.).

The Allahabad Pillar Inscription :

  • It is also known as the Prayag Prashasti, is a eulogy of the Gupta emperor Samudragupta. The inscription was composed by Samudragupta’s court poet and minister, Harishena, and is considered a key historical document of the classical Gupta age.
  • The inscription is inscribed on an Asokan Pillar, which was erected by Ashoka six centuries before Samudragupta. The pillar also contains inscriptions from the 17th century Mughal emperor Jahangir. The inscriptions are difficult to read due to exposure to rain and sunlight.
  • The inscription mentions the conquests of Samudragupta and the boundaries of the Gupta Empire. It also mentions the Davaka kingdom, which was one of five frontier kingdoms of the Gupta Empire.
  • The Anumakonda inscription describes Rudradeva I as the first independent ruler of the Kakatiya kingdom of Warangal.
  • Anumakonda is also the name of an archaeological site.

question: In the Gandhar sculpture the preaching Mudra associated with the Buddha’s First Sermon at Sarnath is........ (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Abhaya
option_2: Dhyana
option_3: Dharmachakra
option_4: Bhumisparsa
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The mudra in the Gandhara sculpture that depicts the Buddha preaching his first sermon at Sarnath is the dharmachakra mudra. The mudra is named for its association with the dharmachakra, or “wheel of law”.
  • In the mudra, the thumb and index finger of both hands touch each other to form a circle, while the other three fingers remain extended.
  • The mudra represents the Buddha’s f irst sermon, called the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, which he gave to his five disciples in a deer park at Sarnath, near Varanasi, after attaining enlightenment.
  • Mudras are symbolic or ritual gestures or poses in Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. They are spiritual gestures employed in the iconography and spiritual practice of Indian religions.

Some mudras of Buddha :

1. Dhyana mudra :

  • One of the five common mudras in Buddhist art. It refers to the prince Siddhartha meditating under the pipal tree before he achieved enlightenment.

2. Bhumisparsha mudra :

  • Also known as the Earth Witness Mudra, this mudra is a symbol of the Buddha reaching enlightenment. It is one of the most common mudras to see in Buddha statues.

3. Abhaya mudra :

  • This mudra is made with the open palm of the right hand, extending outwards at the chest level or slightly higher. Translated from Sanskrit as fearlessness, it signifies “stop”. This means to end fears and phobias that can cloud your mind.

4. Dharmachakra mudra :

  • This mudra is a reference to the Buddha’s f irst sermon at Sarnath. It gets its name from its association with the eponymous dharmachakra or “wheel of law”.

5. Varada mudra :

  • Also known as the “Favourable Mudra” or the Mudra of granting wishes/mercy. This mudra has also been depicted in various Buddha statues.

6. Vitarka mudra :

  • This mudra symbolizes the pursuit of knowledge. Vastu Shastra suggests that placing a Buddha statue with the Vitarka mudra on your library, work, or study desk can help you attain excellence in work and academic pursuits.

7. Anjali mudra :

  • This mudra denotes welcome, prayer, and devotion motion.

8. Uttarabodhi mudra :

  • This mudra denotes the supreme enlightenment through connecting oneself with divine universal energy. It is performed with the help of both the hands, which are placed at the heart with the index f ingers touching and pointing upwards and the remaining fingers intertwined.

question: Toramana belonged to the ethnic horde of the......... (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Scythians
option_2: Hunas
option_3: Yue chis
option_4: Sakas
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Toramana belonged to the Hunas ethnic horde. Toramana was a Huna king who ruled northern India in the late 5th and early 6th century CE.
  • The Hunas were nomadic people from Central Asia who migrated to the Indian subcontinent during the 5th and 6th centuries. They were known for theirmilitary strength and played a significant role in India’s political and cultural history during that period.
  • Toramana was a ruler of the Hephthalite Empire who ruled its Indian region in the late 5th and early 6th century. He consolidated the Hephthalite power in Punjab and conquered northern and central India.

question: Which one of the following important trade centres of ancient india was on the trade route connecting Kalyana with Vengi ? (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Tagara
option_2: Sripura
option_3: Tripuri
option_4: Tamralipti
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The correct answer is (a) Tagara.

  • Tagara, identified with modern Ter in Maharashtra, was one of the most important inland trade centres of ancient India and lay on the trade route that connected Kalyana (modern Basavakalyan in Karnataka) with Vengi (in Andhra Pradesh). It was well-known for trading textiles, precious stones, spices, and luxury goods, making it a hub for merchants along this route. 
  • On the other hand, Sripura (in Chhattisgarh) was more of a political and religious centre under the Panduvamsha dynasty and not directly connected with this trade route. 
  • Tripuri (near Jabalpur in Madhya Pradesh), the capital of the Kalachuri dynasty, too was a political centre of central India rather than a major trade hub on the Kalyana–Vengi route. 
  • Tamralipti (modern Tamluk in West Bengal) was indeed a famous seaport with active overseas trade to Southeast Asia but was associated with the eastern coastal trade network and not the inland route between Kalyana and Vengi.
  • Thus, among the given options, Tagara was the important trade centre situated on this route.

question: The last in the succession of jaina Tirthankaras was......... (UPSC Prelims 1994)
option_1: Parsvanatha
option_2: Rishabha
option_3: Mahavira
option_4: Manisubrata
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Mahavira was the last of the 24 Tirthankaras, or spiritual leaders, in Jainism. Mahavira, also known as Vardhamana, lived in the 6th century BCE. According to tradition, his predecessor, Parshvanatha, lived about 250 years earlier.
  • In Jain philosophy, a Tirthankara is an enlightened soul who is born as a human and attains perfection through intense meditation.
  • In Jainism, a Tirthankara is a spiritual teacher and savior who has crossed the cycle of rebirths and established a path for others to follow. The word “Tirthankara” comes from Sanskrit and translates to “ford-maker”.

It is made up of two parts:

1) “Tirth” which means “ford” and

2) “Kara” which means “Maker”.

  • Jainism believes that each cosmic age produces 24 Tirthankaras. Jains venerate the Tirthankaras with prayers and offerings, and their idols and statues are placed in temples.

Some examples of Tirthankaras include:

1. Mahavira: A savior from the 6th cen tury BCE

2. Rishabhanatha (Adinatha): The first Tirthankara in this cosmic cycle

  • At the end of their human life-span, each Tirthankara achieves liberation (‘moksh’ or ‘nirvan’), ending the cycle of infinite births and deaths.

Parshvanatha :

  • He is the 23rd Tirthankara, or teacher, of Jainism. His name means “beloved of men”. He is also known as Pārśva, Parshva, and Parasnath.
  • In Jainism, Parshvanatha is known as the “Ford-maker,” or savior. He is also known as Nakoda Bhairav because the area is Nakoda. In his lower left hand is the Damaru and in upper left hand is Trishula, which is the same Bhairava found in many Shiva temples.

question: In Sanskrit plays written during the Gupta Period women and sudras speak......... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Sanskrit
option_2: Prakrit
option_3: Pali
option_4: Sauraseni
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • In Sanskrit plays written during the Gupta period, women and Sudras speak Prakrit. The choice of language reflected their social status.
  • The plays depicted men from the upper three varnas speaking Sanskrit, while women and the lower varna-sudra spoke Prakrit.
  • Shauraseni Prakrit was a Middle Indo Aryan language and a Dramatic Prakrit. It was the primary language used in drama in medieval northern India. Shauraseni is said to be the Prakrit most closely related to Classical Sanskrit.

question: The Term Yavanapriya mentioned in ancient Sanskrit text denoted......... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: A fine variety of Indian Muslim
option_2: Ivory
option_3: Damsels sent to the Greek court for dance performance
option_4: Pepper
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The term “Yavanapriya” in ancient Sanskrit texts refers to pepper.
  • The term is made up of two Sanskrit words: “Yavana” which refers to Greeks, and “priya” which means loved.
  • The term refers to the commodity that the Greeks loved from India. Pepper was one of the spices exported from India to the Roman Empire, possibly due to its popularity among the Romans.

question: The name by which Ashoka is generally refered to in his inscription is.......... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Chakravarti
option_2: Dharmadeva
option_3: Dharmakirti
option_4: Priyadarsi
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The name by which Ashoka is generally referred to in his inscriptions is Priyadarshi. Priyadarshi means “He who regards others with kindness”. It is generally used in conjunction with Devanampriya which means “beloved of the Gods”.
  • Ashoka was the third ruler of the Maurya Dynasty and ruled almost the entire Indian subcontinent from c. 268 to 232 BCE.

question: According to Mimamsa system of philosophy liberation is possible by means of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Jnana
option_2: Bhakti
option_3: Yoga
option_4: Karma
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • According to the Mimamsa system of philosophy, liberation is possible by means of karma.
  • Mimamsa is a Sanskrit word that means “reflection” or “critical investigation”. It is a tradition of contemplation that reflects on the meanings of certain Vedic texts.
  • The goal of Mimamsa is to provide enlightenment on dharma. Dharma isunderstood as the set of ritual obligations and prerogatives that, if properly performed, maintains the harmony of the world and furthers the personal goals of the performer.
  • Mimamsa is one of the six astika (orthodox) Indian philosophical school traditions. It is also known as Purva-Mimamsa and Karma-Mimamsa.

question: The concept of Anuvrata was advocated by........... (UPSC Prelims 1995)
option_1: Mahayana Buddhism
option_2: Hinayana Buddhism
option_3: Jainism
option_4: The Lokayata School
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The concept of Anuvrata was advocated by Jainism. Anuvrata is a set of ethical guidelines for laypeople that are less strict than the vows taken by Jain monks and nuns.

It consists of twelve vows, including:

1. Refraining from violence

2. Lying

3. Stealing

4. Sexual misconduct

5. Attachment to possessions

The five fundamental vows of Jainism are:

  • Ahimsa, Satya, Asteya, Brahmacharya, Aparigraha.
  • Anuvrata is seen as a way for laypeople to live a more ethical and meaningful life while still fulfilling their social and familial responsibilities.

1. Mahayana Buddhism : This branch of Buddhism focuses on the idea of helping others reach enlightenment, not just seeking it for oneself. It teach es that anyone can become a Buddha and emphasizes compassion for all beings.

2. Theravada Buddhism (formerly known as Hinayana) : This branchemphasizes following the original teachings of Buddha closely. It focuses on individual enlightenment and the path to becoming an arhat, someone who has achieved enlightenment.

question: The river most mentioned in early Vedic Literature is............ (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Sindhu
option_2: Sutudri
option_3: Sarasvati
option_4: Ganga
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The river most mentioned in early Vedic literature is the Sindhu. The Sindhu is also known as the Indus River, and it is one of the longest rivers in Asia. It flows through India, Pakistan, and China.
  • The Sindhu is mentioned over 150 times in the Rig Veda, the oldest known Hindu text. Some say that Hinduism originated on the banks of the Sindhu.
  • The river Sarasvati is the most prominent river in the Rig Veda, and is prayed to almost 75 times in the Rigveda Samhitā.

question: Who among the following is known for his work on medicine during the Gupta period?  (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Saumilla
option_2: Sudraka
option_3: Shaunaka
option_4: Susrutha
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Sushruta is known for his work on medicine during the Gupta period.
  • Sushruta is considered the “Father of Plastic Surgery” and is known as the “Father of Indian Medicine” for inventing and developing surgical procedures.
  • He lived in India between 1000 and 800 BC and is responsible for the advancement of medicine in ancient India.
  • Sushruta is also known for emphasizing the importance of hygiene and aseptic practices during surgical procedures. He recognized the risk of infection and prescribed measures to prevent it, such as sterilizing instruments, cleaning wounds, and maintaining a clean surgical environment.
  • The Gupta age in ancient India has been called the “Golden Age of India” because of the many accomplishments in the field of arts, science, and literature that Indians made under the Guptas.

Saumilla :

  • He was a dramatist and physician who lived during the Gupta period (320–550 AD). Saumilla is also considered an ancient Sanskrit poet, along with Bhāsa and Kaviputra.

Sudraka :

  • He was an ancient Indian king and playwright who lived in the first or second century AD.

He is credited with writing three Sanskrit plays:

1. Mrichchhakatika (The Little Clay Cart)

2. Vinavasavadatta and

3. Padmaprabhritaka

  • According to the play’s prologue, Sudraka was a Kshatriya king who was brave, handsome, and knowledgeable about Rigveda, Samaveda, and mathematics. He was also a devotee of Lord Shiva and had performed the Asvamedha sacrifice.

Shaunaka :

  • In Vedic texts, Shaunaka Rishi’s full name is Indrotdaivaya Shaunaka. He was a Vedic teacher and the son of the sage Bhriguvanshi Shunak. Shaunaka Rishi ran a Gurukul with ten thousand students and was honored as kulapati (chancellor).
  • In the Vishnu Purana, Shaunaka is the son of Gritsamada and is said to have invented the system of the four levels of human life. The Vishnu Purana also describes Sūta mahamuni narrating mythological stories to a group of sages headed by Shaunaka maha muni.

question:
Which of the following were common to both Buddhism and Jainism? (UPSC Prelims 1996)

1. Avoidance of extremities of penance and enjoyment

2. Indifference to the authority of the Vedas

3. Denial of efficacy of rituals

4. Non injury to animal life

Select the correct answer using the codes given below :
option_1: 1, 2, 3 and 4
option_2: 2, 3 and 4
option_3: 1, 3 and 4
option_4: 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Buddhism and Jainism share many similarities, including:

1. Karma :

  • Both religions believe in karma, which is the attachment of positive and negative forces to the soul.

2. Salvation :

  • Both religions believe that salvation can only be achieved through nirvana.

3. Helping others :

  • Both religions believe in helping others and teaching their values to others.

4. Materialistic world :

  • Both religions teach to stay away from the materialistic world and desires.

5. Moral lives :

  • Both religions give importance to the moral lives of people.

6. Harm to humans :

  • The teachings of Jainism and Buddhism condemn all sorts of harm meted to humans or living beings.

7. Non-violence :

  • Both religions believe and propagate non violence, good social conduct, charity, and generosity.

8. Atheism :

  • Both religions are atheistic in the sense that they don’t believe in there’s a creator god or any super being.

9. Common background :

  • Both religions have a common background associated with the Aryan Culture.

10. Against Vedas :

  • Both religions were against Vedas and the Vedic culture as well as against Brahmanism.

11. Denial of efficiency of rituals :

  • Both religions deny the efficiency of rituals.

12. Only those who left their homes could gain true knowledge :

  • Both religions believe that only those who left their homes could gain true knowledge.

13. Non injury to animal life :

 Buddhism and Jainism also share some other similarities, including:

1. Both religions emerged as a new religious ideas from late 6th century BCE.

2. Both religions preached Satya, Ahimsa, Brahmacharya, and detachment from the materialistic world.

question:
Match List I with list II and select the correct answer by using codes given below the list :  (UPSC Prelims 1996)                                                             

List I

List II

A. Visakhadatta

1. Medicine

B. Varahamihira

2. Drama

C. Charaka

3. Astronomy

D. Brahamagupta

4. Mathematics

 

 

 

 

 

 

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 1 3 4 2
option_2: 2 1 3 4
option_3: 2 3 1 4
option_4: 3 4 1 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Visakhadatta :

  • Visakhadatta was an important Sanskrit playwright of ancient India.
  • His most famous work is Mudrarakshasa, a political drama set in the period of Chandragupta Maurya and his minister Chanakya, which portrays the establishment of Mauryan rule.
  • Another of his works, Devichandraguptam, though only partially surviving, narrates events from the Gupta dynasty.
  • His plays stand out for their historical-political themes, unlike most Sanskrit plays which were based on romance or mythology. This makes him a key figure in the literary tradition.

Varahamihira :

  • Varahamihira (505–587 CE) was one of the "Navaratnas" (Nine Gems) in the court of Chandragupta II. He was both an astronomer and an astrologer.
  • His most significant works include Brihat Samhita, an encyclopedic text dealing with astronomy, astrology, architecture, omens, and weather, and Panchasiddhantika, which summarized five earlier astronomical treatises.
  • Varahamihira accurately predicted many celestial events and emphasized the role of mathematical calculations in astronomy.

Charaka :

  • Charaka was a legendary physician and one of the principal contributors to Ayurveda, the ancient Indian system of medicine.
  • He is credited with writing the Charaka Samhita, a comprehensive text dealing with internal medicine, diagnosis, physiology, and pharmacology.
  • The text also discusses concepts of health, disease prevention, and the role of diet and lifestyle.
  • Charaka emphasized the importance of ethics for physicians and described the human body as a combination of the three doshas — Vata, Pitta, and Kapha. His work remains a cornerstone of Ayurveda even today.

Brahmagupta :

  • Brahmagupta (598–668 CE) was one of the greatest mathematicians and astronomers of ancient India, best known for his treatise Brahmasphutasiddhanta.
  • He was the first to systematically explain the concept of zero as a number, rules for negative numbers, operations with surds, and algebraic identities.
  • He also worked on geometry, trigonometry, and methods of solving quadratic equations.
  • In astronomy, he dealt with planetary motions and eclipses.
  • His mathematical innovations were later transmitted to the Islamic world and then to Europe, profoundly influencing global mathematics.

Hence, the correct sequence of matching A, B, C, and D with List II is 2, 3, 1, 4.

question: In the context of ancient Indian society, which one of the following terms does not belong to the category of the other three? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Kula
option_2: Vamsa
option_3: Kosa
option_4: Gotra
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • In the context of ancient Indian society, Kosa is not related to the other three terms, which are related to family:

1. Kula :

  • A Sanskrit word that can mean “community,” “clan,” or “tribe”. In Hinduism, it can refer to a part of a village or a family member.

2. Vamsa : 

  • A Sanskrit word that means “family” or “lineage”. It can also refer to a genre of ancient and medieval literature in Buddhism, Hinduism, and Jainism.

3. Gotra :

  • In Hinduism, it is commonly considered to be equivalent to clan. It refers to people who are descendants in an unbroken male line from a common male ancestor. 

4. Kosa :

  • is a term used for treasury. It can also refer to a unit of measurement, such as a unit of currency or the value of wealth.

question: Which one of the following texts of ancient India allows divorce to a wife deserted by her husband? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Kamasutra
option_2: Manavadharmashastra
option_3: Sukra Nitisara
option_4: Arthashastra
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Arthashastra is an ancient Indian text that allows a wife to seek divorce if she has been abandoned by her husband.
  • The Arthashastra is a Sanskrit treatise on statecraft, political science, economic policy, and military strategy. It is traditionally attributed to Kautilya, also known as Chanakya and Vishnugupta. The text is likely the work of multiple authors over centuries, and was influential until the 12th century. It was rediscovered in 1905 and published in 1909.
  • According to Hindu law, marriage creates an indissoluble bond between the husband and wife. However, the Arthashastra allows divorce in the Hindu religion.

Kama Sutra :

  • It is an ancient Indian Sanskrit text written by Vātsyāyana, a celibate Indian philosopher. It’s considered one of the world’s oldest textbooks of erotic love and is widely considered to be the standard work on human sexual behavior in Sanskrit literature.
  • The Kama Sutra is made up of seven books with 36 chapters. The Kama Sutra is written as a guide to the “art-of-living” well, the nature of love, finding a life partner, maintaining one’s love life, and other aspects pertaining to pleasure-oriented faculties of human life. It’s not exclusively or predominantly a sex manual on sex positions.

Manava-Dharmashastra :

  • It is a Sanskrit term that means something in Hinduism. It is also known as Manusmriti or the Laws of Manu. The Manusmriti is a legal text and constitution in the Dharmaśāstra literary tradition of Hinduism. It is written in sloka verses, which are two non-rhyming lines of 16 syllables each. The Manusmriti is traditionally considered the most authoritative of the books of the Hindu code (Dharma-shastra) in India.
  • The Manusmriti is an ancient legal text or ‘dharmashastra’ of Hinduism that describes the social system from the time of the Aryans. It was composed sometime between the 2nd century BCE and 3rd century CE. The Manusmriti is attributed to the legendary first man and lawgiver, Manu.

The Manusmriti prescribes 10 essential rules for the observance of dharma, including:

1. Patience (dhriti)

2. Forgiveness (kshama)

3. Piety, or self control (dama)

4. Honesty (asteya)

5. Sanctity (shauch)

6. Control of senses (indraiya-nigrah)

7. Reason (dhi)

8. Knowledge or learning (vidya)

9. Truthfulness (satya)

10. Absence of anger (krodha)

Shukra-Nitisara :

  • It is a Sanskrit term that refers to a work on ethics by Śukrācārya. It’s also known as rājakrtyakathana, which refers to the duties and responsibilities of a king.
  • Shukra-Nitisara is considered as Shukracharya’s System of Morals and is part of Dharmasastra. It is a treatise on the science of governance, structured towards upholding the morals through implementing theories of political science.

question: Which one of the following was a saiva sect in ancient India? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Ajivika
option_2: Mattamayura
option_3: Mayamata
option_4: Isanasivagurudevapaddhati
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Mattamayura was a Shaiva sect that flourished in central and northern India, particularly around the 9th–10th centuries CE.
  • It was associated with the worship of Shiva and promoted Shaiva religious traditions, temples, and monastic institutions, making it a distinct Shaiva sect.

Ajivika :

  • It was an ancient heterodox religious order that arose around the same time as Buddhism and Jainism.
  • Founded by Makkhali Gosala, the Ajivikas believed in strict determinism and the doctrine of niyati (fate), and they did not follow Shaivism. 

Mayamata :

  • It is a Sanskrit treatise on Vastu Shastra (Hindu architecture and town planning), dealing with the design of temples, houses, and settlements, so it cannot be considered a Shaiva sect. 

Isanasivagurudevapaddhati :

  • It is a later work on Shaiva rituals and practices composed in Kerala, but it is a text rather than a religious sect.

Thus, out of the given options, only Mattamayura represents a Shaiva sect, while the others belong to different philosophical or literary traditions.

question: Who among the following was a Brahmavadini who composed some hymns of the Vedas? (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: Lopamudra
option_2: Gargi
option_3: Leelavati
option_4: Savitri
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Lopamudra :

  • She was a Brahmavadini, a woman who pursued Vedic studies and composed hymns.
  • Lopamudra, the wife of sage Agastya, is credited with composing hymns in the Rigveda, which express both philosophical thought and personal emotions. This shows that women in the Vedic age could also be composers and participants in intellectual discourse.

Gargi :

  • She was also a famous Brahmavadini, but instead of composing hymns, she was known for her philosophical debates in the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad, particularly with Yajnavalkya, on the nature of reality and the universe.
  • She was celebrated more for her intellectual reasoning than for hymn composition. 

Leelavati : 

  • She is not connected with the Vedic period at all, she is associated with mathematics.
  • The famous text Leelavati, written by Bhaskara II in the 12th century CE, deals with arithmetic and geometry, and though named after his daughter, she herself was not a composer of Vedic hymns. 

Savitri :

  • She is a revered figure in Hindu tradition, known mainly from the Mahabharata and the story of her devotion and wisdom in saving her husband Satyavan’s life, but she is not credited with composing hymns of the Vedas.

Thus, among the given options, Lopamudra stands out as the Brahmavadini who actually composed hymns of the Vedas.

question: The word “Hindu” as reference to the people of Hind (India) was first used by.......... (UPSC Prelims 1996)
option_1: The Greeks
option_2: The Romans
option_3: The chinese
option_4: The Arabs
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The word "Hindu" was first used by the Greeks to refer to the people and country that lived beyond the Indus river. The word "Hindu" or "Indu" was used by the Greeks.
  • The word "Hind" is the Persian equivalent of Iranian Sindh, which refers to the Indo Aryans. "Stan" in Persian means "land" or "country". The word "Hindu" was also used by the Persians, perhaps between 1000 and 500 BCE.
  • In the 18th century, European merchants and colonists began to refer to the followers of Indian religions collectively as Hindus.
  • The Greeks, or Hellenes, are an ethnic group and nation native to Greece and other countries around the Mediterranean and Black Sea.
  • The Romans gave the name "Greeks" to the people of Greece. The Greeks called themselves "Hellenes" and their land "Hellas".
  • The Romans were a civilization that originated in the Italian Peninsula and ruled many countries across Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa. The Romans spoke Latin, which became the language of administration, legislation, and the military throughout the classical period.
  • The Chinese came into contact with India mainly through Buddhism, beginning around the 1st century CE and continuing strongly from the 4th to 7th centuries CE.
  • When Chinese pilgrims and scholars such as Faxian (Fa-Hien), Xuanzang (Hsüan-tsang), and Yijing (I-tsing) travelled to India, they recorded the name of the country using transliterations of the Sanskrit or local Indian names, but not the word “Hindu.”
  • Arabs are people who are native to the Middle East and North Africa and speak Arabic. They are descendants of the first Eurasian populations, who migrated out of Africa between 125,000 and 60,000 years ago.

question:
Match list I with List II and select the correct answer by using the codes given below the list : (UPSC Prelims 1997)

List - I

 List - II

A. Gupta 

1. Badami

B. Chandella

2. Panamalai

C. Chalukya

3. Khajuraho

D. Pallava

4. Deogarh

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 4 3 1 2
option_2: 4 2 3 1
option_3: 2 3 4 1
option_4: 3 4 1 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Each of these sites reflects the distinctive architectural styles and artistic contributions of their respective dynasties.
  • The Guptas are associated with Deogarh, where the Dashavatara Temple dedicated to Vishnu represents one of the earliest and finest examples of Gupta structural temple architecture. 
  • The Chandellas built the world-famous temples of Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh, known for their exquisite Nagara-style design and intricate sculptures. 
  • The Chalukyas had their capital at Badami, famous for rock-cut cave temples and early Dravidian-style shrines. 
  • The Pallavas, who pioneered South Indian temple architecture, are linked with Panamalai, where the Talagirisvara Temple built by Narasimhavarman II showcases early Dravidian features. 

Therefore the correct answer is (A) 4 3 1 2.

question:
The following maps shows four of the sixsteen mahajanapandas that existed in ancient India...........   (UPSC Prelims 1997)

The Places marked A, B, C and D respectively are :
option_1: Matsya, Cedi, Kosala, Anga
option_2: Surasena, Avanti, Vatsa, Magadha
option_3: Matsya, Avanti, Vatsa, Anga
option_4: Surasena, Cedi, Kosala, Magadha
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The correct answer to this question is option 3: Matsya, Avanti, Vatsa, Anga. Each of these names corresponds to one of the sixteen great kingdoms, or mahajanapadas, that existed in ancient India.

The Mahajanapadas :

  • There were 16 kingdoms that existed in ancient India from 600–345 BCE. The word “Mahajanapadas” comes from the Sanskrit words maha and janapada, which means “great realm”.
  • The Mahajanapadas were located in the northern and eastern parts of India, including the modern-day Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Haryana, and Madhya Pradesh. They were known for their advanced systems of administration, trade, and warfare.
  • The Mahajanapadas were ruled by kings and were known for their advanced systems of administration, trade, and warfare. The Mahajanapadas practiced Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism, and their common languages were Prakritis, Sanskrit, and Pali. The two types of government during the Mahajanapadas were monarchies and republics.

The 16 Mahajanapadas were:

  • Anga, Aśmaka, Avanti, Chedi, Gandhāra, Kamboja, Kāśi, Kosala, Kuru, Magadha, Malla, Matsya, Pañcāla, Śūrasena, Vaji, Vatsa (or Vatsa).

question: Which one of the following edicts mention the peronal name of Asoka?  (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Kalsi
option_2: Rummindei
option_3: Special Kalinga Edict
option_4: Maski
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Maski and Gurjara edicts mention the personal name of Ashoka.
  • The Maski inscription, found in 1915, is the first edict of Emperor Ashoka to contain his name. The Maski version of Minor Rock Edict No. 1 is historically significant because it confirms the association of the title “Devanampriya” with the name “Asoka”.
  • The Edicts of Ashoka are a group of more than 30 inscriptions associated with the Mauryan emperor Ashoka. These inscriptions are found on various structures like pillars, cave walls, and boulders.
  • Ashoka’s Rock Edicts promoted non violence and respect for life, which are important aspects of the Buddhism religion.

question: The silver coins issued by the Guptas were called..........  (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Rupaka
option_2: Karshapana
option_3: Dinara
option_4: Pana
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The silver coins issued by the Guptas were called Rupaka. These coins were found in large numbers in Rajasthan’s Bharatpur and most of the northern parts of the country.
  • Chandragupta II was the first Gupta ruler to issue silver coins, which were called Rupaka. He was the son of Samudragupta and also known as the Chandragupta Vikramaditya.
  • 16 Rupaka was equal to 1 gold coin.
  • In ancient India, Karshapanas, Panas, and Dinaras were silver coins. The Mahajanapadas, or Republic kingdoms, introduced these coins in the 6th century BC. The coins were made by flattening silver and pressing seals on them. Different kingdoms had different symbols and sculptures inscribed on the coins.
  • The name Pana comes from the word pani, which means “hand”. The name means “handful” and is based on the weight of 80 rati seeds, which is about 9.5 grams.
  • The Sanskrit word Dīnāra is related to the Prakrit word Dīnāra. It can mean a gold ornament, a seal, a weight, or a dinar. The word Dinar is mentioned in the Indian texts Harivansh and Mahavircharit.

question: In the mahayana Buddhism, the Bodhisattva Avalokitesvara was also known as..........  (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Vajrapani
option_2: Manjusri
option_3: Padmapani
option_4: Maitreya
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • In Mahayana Buddhism, the Bodhisattva Avalokitesvara is also known as Padmapani.Padmapani is a Sanskrit name that translates to “holder of the lotus”.
  • Padmapani is a favored form of Avalokiteshvara, the embodiment of Buddhist compassion. Padmapani is often depicted holding a lotus flower in his left hand, and with a small figure of the Buddha Amitabha on his head.
  • Padmapani is one of the most revered deities in Buddhism, especially in Mahayana Buddhism. He is associated with the power of Buddha, and is often depicted as a compassionate bodhisattva who can alleviate the suffering of sentient beings.

Vajrapani :

  • Its a nonhuman attendant of the Buddha in Mahayana Buddhist mythology. His name means “Holding a/the Vajra in his Hand”. Vajrapani is one of the earliest-appearing bodhisattvas in Mahayana Buddhism. He is the protector and guide of Gautama Buddha and rose to symbolize the Buddha’s power. Vajrapani was originally a Yakṣa, but later became a Bodhisattva.
  • He is considered one of the earliest of the three protective deities surrounding the Buddha and one of the major dharmapalas (protectors of dharma) in Mahayana Buddhism. Vajrapani is also a Buddhist savior who holds a thunderbolt.
  • The name Vajrapani comes from the word “vajra”, which means “thunderbolt”. In early Buddhist iconography, the thunderbolt scepter had a meaning associated with clarity of pure thought.

In Mahāyāna Buddhism, Mañjuśrī :

  • It is a bodhisattva who represents the Buddhas’ transcendent wisdom. His name translates to “Gentle Glory” in Sanskrit. He is also known as Mãnjughosa (“Sweet Voice”) and Vāgīśvara (“Lord of Speech”). Mañjuśrī is one of the oldest and most important bodhisattvas in the Indian Mahayana Buddhist pantheon.
  • He is closely related to the wisdom goddess Prajnaparamita, and both first appeared in north Indian religious texts and imagery in the eighth century.
  • Mañjuśrī is a popular Buddhist savior figure in Licchavi Nepal.Mañjuśrī is the protector of the Buddha or Tathagata family, which is led by Vairocana. He is sometimes depicted sitting on a lion, which is also the animal associated with Vairocana.

In ancient India, Maitreya :

  • He is the Buddha of the future who will help humans achieve enlightenment. Maitreya is a bodhisattva, which is a spiritual being on the path to enlightenment. According to Mahayana teachings, a Buddha is first born as a bodhisattva, and then after many lifetimes, progresses on to Buddhahood. Maitreya is the earliest bodhisattva around whom a cult developed and is mentioned in scriptures from the 3rd century CE. He is accepted by all schools of Buddhism and is still the only bodhisattva generally honored by the Theravada tradition.
  • Maitreya is typically pictured seated, with either both feet on the ground or crossed at the ankles, on a throne. He is dressed in the clothes of either a bhikhu (monk) or Indian royalty.
  • Maitreya can be identified by the lip of the sacred water flask in his left hand and by his loop topknot, a hairstyle common to Maitreya images.

question: Which one of the following was a corporation of merchants in ancient India?  (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Chaturvedimangalam
option_2: Parishad
option_3: Ashtadikgaja
option_4: Manigrama
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Manigrama was a corporation of merchants in ancient India. It was a large and influential guild of South Indian merchants from the 10th–12th centuries CE.
  • Manigrama was a medieval merchant guild organized by itinerant ethnic Indian traders. It functioned like a guild, and was an organization of traders, often operating within a specific business or industry.They were quite prevalent in ancient India, facilitating commerce and trade.
  • In ancient India, a shreni was an association of traders, merchants, and artisans. Generally, a separate shreni existed for a particular group of persons engaged in the same vocation or activity.

question:
Match List I with list II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the list : (UPSC Prelims 1997)

List I 

List II

 A. Varahamihira

1. Prabandha Chintamani

 B. Visakhadatta

2. Mrchchhakatikam

 C. Sudraka

3. Brhat Samhita

 D. Bilhana

4. Devi Chandraguptam

 

5. Vikramankadevacharita

Codes :

A B C D
option_1: 3 4 5 2
option_2: 3 4 2 5
option_3: 5 3 4 1
option_4: 1 3 5 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Varahamihira :

  • He was one of the greatest scholars of the Gupta period, flourishing during the reign of Chandragupta II. He was an astronomer, astrologer, and polymath whose most famous work, the “Brhat Samhita,” is an encyclopedic Sanskrit text.
  • It covers an impressive range of subjects — from planetary movements, eclipses, and weather forecasting to architecture, gemology, water divining, and rituals. This work beautifully blends scientific observation with traditional beliefs, reflecting the intellectual vibrancy of the Gupta era.

Visakhadatta : 

  • He was a renowned Sanskrit playwright best known for his political dramas. His famous work “Devi Chandraguptam” narrates the story of Chandragupta Maurya’s rise to power, describing how he overthrew the Nanda dynasty with the guidance of Chanakya. Though only fragments of the play survive, it provides valuable insights into Mauryan history and statecraft.
  • Visakhadatta’s other well-known work, “Mudrarakshasa,” also deals with similar political intrigue and administrative strategy, showing his skill in blending drama with historical realism.

Sudraka : 

  • Sudraka is credited with writing the famous Sanskrit play “Mrichchhakatikam” (The Little Clay Cart), which is one of the earliest examples of classical Indian drama depicting urban life and human emotions in a realistic manner. Unlike other courtly or mythological dramas, it focuses on the lives of ordinary people — merchants, courtesans, and Brahmins.
  • The story revolves around Charudatta, a poor Brahmin, and Vasantasena, a courtesan, portraying themes of love, morality, and social justice. The play is remarkable for its vivid portrayal of ancient Indian society, humor, and emotional depth.

Bilhana :

  • He was a Kashmiri poet of the 11th century CE, served in the court of the Western Chalukya king Vikramaditya VI. His court epic “Vikramankadevacharita” is a mahakavya composed in praise of his patron. It narrates the life, conquests, and virtues of Vikramaditya VI in an ornate poetic style.
  • The work is significant not only for its literary excellence but also as a historical source on the Chalukya dynasty and South Indian politics of the period. Bilhana is also known for his lyrical work “Caurapanchashika”, which expresses romantic and emotional themes.
  • Thus, each pairing represents a key figure of India’s classical literary and scientific tradition — Varahamihira symbolizing intellectual science, Visakhadatta the art of political drama, Sudraka the spirit of social realism, and Bilhana the refinement of courtly epic poetry — making the correct code (b) 3 4 2 5.

question: Which one of the following is not a part of early Jaina Literature?  (UPSC Prelims 1997)
option_1: Therigatha
option_2: Acarangasutra
option_3: Sutrakritanga
option_4: Brihatkalpasutra
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Therigatha is not a part of early Jaina literature. It is a Buddhist scripture, a collection of short poems supposedly recited by early members of the Buddhist Sangha in India around 600 BC.
  • Therigatha is considered a part of the Theravada Buddhist literature, not Jain literature.

The Acaranga Sutra :

  • It is a religious text in Jainism. It is the first of the 12 Angas, which are part of the agamas, or religious texts. The Acaranga Sutra is made up of two books, with the first book dating from around the 5th–4th century BCE and the second book from around the late 4th–2nd century BCE. The Acaranga Sutra is written in Ardhamagadhi Prakrit.
  • The Acaranga Sutra is the first text that Jain monks study. It describes the behavior of an ascetic, including their conduct, clothing, alms, and mode of walking and speaking. It also describes the penance of Lord Mahavir.

Sutrakritanga :

  • It is a book that is the second agama of the 12 main Angas of the Jain canons. It is also known as the Golden Book of Jainism.
  • Sutrakritanga is a popular guide for young monks. It is traditionally dated to the 4th-century BCE and consists of two parts (verse and prose) explaining various doctrinal aspects of Jainism. Sutrakritanga is also known in Prakrit as Sūyagadamga.
  • According to the Svetambara tradition, it was written by Gandhara Sudharmasvami in Ardhamagadhi Prakrit.

Brihatkalpasutra :

  • It is a cultural study. Kalpasutra is a Jain text that contains biographies of the Jain Tirthankaras, including Parshvanatha and Mahavira. It is considered the most important canonical text in Jain literature for the Svetambaras, one of the two sects of Jainism.
  • The Kalpasutra is traditionally ascribed to Bhadrabahu, which would place it in the 4th century BCE. It was probably written 980 or 993 years after the Nirvana (Moksha) of Mahavira.

 The Kalpasutra is divided into three parts:

1. the Kalpasutra

2. the Grihyasutra and

3. the Dharmasutra

question:
Assertion (A) : According to Asokas edicts social harmony among the people was more important than religious devolion. 

Reason (R) : He spread ideas of equity instead of promotion of religion   (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true, but R is a not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
  • The Edicts of Ashoka are a collection of more than 30 inscriptions on boulders, cave walls, and the Pillars of Ashoka.
  • The inscriptions are attributed to Emperor Ashoka, who ruled the Maurya Empire from 268 BCE to 232 BCE. The edicts are located in public places and focus on social and moral precepts, rather than specific religious practices.
  • The edicts emphasize social harmony by spreading ideas of equity instead of promoting religion. The edicts also promote the essentials of all religions and encourage religious dialogue and concord.
  • One of the edicts states that people who praise their own religion and condemn others only harm their own religion. The edict concludes by admonishing people to listen to and respect the doctrines of others.

question:
Assertion (A): The Gandhara School of art bears the mark of Hellenstic influence

Reason (R): Hinayana from was influenced by that art (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Both A and R true, and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R true, but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true, but R is false
option_4: A is false, but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The statement “The Gandhara School of art bears the mark of Hellenistic influence” is true, but the statement “Hinayana form was influenced by that art” is false.
  • The Gandhara School of Art is a combination of Greco-Roman and Indian styles. It was influenced by the Indian Mathura School of Art, and also drew inspiration from the art of ancient Greece and Rome. Some say the Gandhara School of Art is Hellenistic, with features like dynamic animation, contracted brows, and open mouths.
  • Hinayana Buddhism is a form of Buddhism that follows the core teachings of Buddha, and seeks individual salvation through meditation and consciousness. The term “Hīnayāna” literally means “small/deficient vehicle” or “small path”.

question: Which one of the following ancient Indian records is the earliest royal order to preserve foodgrains to be utilised during the crises in the country? (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Sohagaura Copper plate
option_2: Rummindei pillar edict of Ashoka
option_3: Prayag -Prasasti
option_4: Mehrauli Pillar inscription of chandra
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Sohagaura copper plate is the earliest royal order in ancient India to preserve foodgrains for use during crises.
  • The plate is from the Maurya period and is written in Brahmi script. The inscription records a royal order to distribute grains carefully, which could be due to famine.
  • The Sohagaura copper plate inscription is an Indian copper plate inscription written in Prakrit language of Brahmi script. It highlights the proactive approach of the Gupta kings in managing food resources and ensuring the well-being of the people during challenging times.
  • It is not a Harappan site. It is an Ashokan era copper plate inscription written in Prakrit in the Brahmi script.

The Rummindei Pillar Inscription :

  • It also known as the Lumbini Pillar Edict, is a famous edict written by Ashoka in Lumbini, Nepal. The inscription describes Ashoka’s visit to Lumbini, the birthplace of Gautama Buddha, in the 21st year of his reign. The inscription also mentions that Ashoka exempted Lumbini from paying any further taxes and reduced the taxes of the people living in the area.
  • The Rummindei Edict is one of the Minor Pillar Edicts of Ashoka. It also marks the f irst time in historical records that Ashoka uses the epithet “Sakyamuni” (Sage of the Shakyas) to describe the historical Buddha.
  • Ashoka’s pillars were often hundreds of miles apart and could weigh up to 50 tons each.
  • The Mughal Empire founders later moved several of the pillars, removing the animal capitals.

Mehrauli iron pillar inscription :

  • It is from the Gupta period and describes the authority of Chandragupta Vikramaditya over northwest India. The inscription is generally agreed to refer to the Guptaemperor Chandragupta II (375-413 AD).
  • The inscription describes a king named Chandra as a devotee of the god Vishnu. It also records the erection of a pillar of Vishnu on a hill called Vishnupad, which means “hill of the footprint of Vishnu”.
  • The inscription states that the southern ocean is “perfumed by the breezes” of Chandra’s prowess. This may be a reference to Chandragupta’s extension of the Gupta rule to the Arabian Sea after his conquest of the Western Kshatrapa territory.
  • The inscription also describes a king named Chandra “on whose arm fame was inscribed by the sword”.
  • The iron pillar is 23 feet 8 inches (7.2 meters) high and 16 inches in diameter. It is made of 98% wrought iron and has remained rust-free for a long time due to its high phosphorus content and lack of sulfur and magnesium.

question:
Which of the following pairs are correctly matched ? (UPSC Prelims 1998)

  1. Lothal  :   Ancient dockyard
  2. Sarnath  : First sermon of Buddha
  3. Rajgir  :   Lion capital of Asoka
  4. Nalanda : Great seat of Buddhist learning

Select the correct answer using the codes given below

Codes :
option_1: 1, 2, 3 and 4
option_2: 3 and 4
option_3: 1, 2 and 4
option_4: 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Here are some correct matches:

1. Sarnath: The first sermon of Buddha

2. Nalanda: Great seat of Buddhist learning

3. Lothal: Ancient dockyard

Here’s some more information about these matches:

1. Sarnath: In 528 BCE, Gautama Bud dha delivered his first sermon at Sar nath, near the confluence of the Gan ges and Varuna rivers. The Buddha mentioned Sarnath as one of four pilgrimage sites for his followers. The Lion Capital of Ashoka, which com memorates the first sermon, is lo cated at Sarnath. The capital features four Asiatic lions seated back to back, and is considered one of the finest ex amples of Mauryan period sculpture. After independence, the capital was adopted as the National Emblem of India.

2. Lothal: Lothal is an ancient Indus site in Gujarat on the Gulf of Combay. The National Institute of Oceanogra phy discovered marine microfossils and salt and gypsum crystals in the structure, indicating that it was once a dockyard.

3. Nalanda: A great seat of Buddhist learning

4. Rajgir : It is a city in India that is known for being the f irst capital of the Magadha kingdom. The Magadha kingdom eventually became the Mauryan Empire. Rajgir is also mentioned in the Mahabharata, India’s most famous literary epic.

  • Rajgir is also important in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain scriptures. It was the capital of the Haryanka, Pradyota, and Brihadratha dynasties, as well as the Mauryan Empire.
  • Rajgir is also the location of the ancient university of Nalanda.
  • The Rajgir Hills are a historical and religious center for Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains. The hills include the ruins of a jail where Bimbisara was murdered by his son Ajatashatru.

question: The concept of Eight- fold path forms the theme of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1998)
option_1: Dipavamsa
option_2: Divyavadana
option_3: Mahaparinibban Sutta
option_4: Dharma Chakara Pravartana Sutta
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The concept of the Eightfold Path is the theme of the Dharma Chakara Pravartana Sutta. The Eightfold Path is a central concept in Buddhism and a core teaching of the Buddha. It is a path that leads to the cessation of suffering and the attainment of enlightenment.
  • The idea of the Eightfold Path appears in what is regarded as the first sermon of the founder of Buddhism, Siddhartha Gautama, known as the Buddha.
  • In Buddhist symbolism, the Noble Eightfold Path is often represented by means of the Dharma wheel (dharmachakra), in which its eight spokes represent the eight elements of the path.
  • The Eightfold Path contains the famous “Pancha-Sila” – the five vows for desisting from killing, stealing, sensuality, lying, and intoxication.

The Dipavamsa and Mahavamsa :

  • They are Sri Lankan Buddhist works written in Pali. Both works provide historical and mythical accounts of the Buddha’s life, Buddhist Councils, Asoka, and the arrival of Buddhism to Sri Lanka.
  • The Dipavamsa is the oldest extant historical record of Sri Lanka, compiled in the 4th century. It is considered to be one of the main sources for the later and more comprehensive historical chronicle, the Mahavamsa. The Mahavamsa is considered to be the oldest and longest chronicle in the world. While portions of the poem may be considered more rooted in folklore and myth, there is also factual information.
  • The Dipavamsa is believed to have been the first Pali text composed entirely in Ceylon. A subsequent work sometimes known as Culavamsa extends the Mahavamsa to cover the period from the reign of Mahasena of Anuradhapura (277–304 CE) until 1815.

The Divyāvadāna :

  • It is a Sanskrit anthology of Buddhist narratives. The title translates to “Divine or “Heavenly Stories/Exploits/Narratives”. The anthology contains 36 Buddhist avadānas (narratives) and two sūtras. The stories celebrate the lives of exemplary figures in Buddhist history. The anthology also authenticates local dharma traditions and dramatizes the importance of moral discipline, karma (action), and dĀna (giving).
  • The Divyāvadāna may date back to the 2nd century CE. Many of the tales originate in Mūlasarvāstivādin vinaya texts. The stories express the moral and ethical impulses of Indian Buddhist thought.
  • The Divyāvadāna is a Buddhist work from Nepal. The title is a Sanskrit compound consisting of the terms divya and avadāna.

The Mahaparinibbana Sutta :

  • It is a Buddhist scripture that describes the Buddha’s final year, his death, and his activities and teachings. It is the 16th and longest discourse in the Collection of Long Discourses (Dīgha Nikāya) of the Pāli Canon.
  • The Mahaparinibbana Sutta translates to “Discourse on the Great Final Extinction”. It is considered one of the oldest texts in the canon, though it contains later interpolations.
  • The Mahaparinibbana Sutta is a rich source of details about the Buddha as a person, and how the Buddhist community coped with his passing.

option_1: Kaniska at the times of his death
option_2: Samudragupta after the close if his south indian campaing
