question: The ancient Indian Play Mudrarakshasa of Visakhadutt has its subject on........... (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: A conflict between gods and demons of ancient Hindu lore
option_2: A romantic story of an Aryan Prince and a tribal woman
option_3: The story of the power struggle between two aryan tribes
option_4: The court intrigues at the time of Chandragupta Maurya
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Mudrarakshasa is a historical play in Sanskrit by Vishakhadatta that narrates the ascent of the king Chandragupta Maurya to power in India.
  • The historical authenticity of the Mudrarakshasa is somewhat supported by the description of this period of history in Classical Hellenistic sources: the violent rule of the Nanda, the usurpation of Chandragupta, the formation of the Maurya Empire, and the various battles with the kingdoms of the Northwest resulting from the conquests of Alexander the Great.
  • The title Mudrarakshasa refers to the signet ring of Rakshasa which was stolen by Chanakya’s spy.
  • Chanakya was reportedly the chief minister to the Chandragupta Maurya. The Mudrarakshasa is written in Sanskrit language.

question: In ancient Indian Buddhist Monasteries a ceremony called Pavarana used to be held. It was the......... (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: Occasion to elect the Saṅghaparināyaka and two speakers, one on Dhamma and the other on Vinaya
option_2: Confession by monks of their offences committed during their stay in the monasteries during the rainy season
option_3: Ceremony of initiation of new persons into the Buddhist Saṅgha in which the head is shaved and yellow robes are offered
option_4: Gathering of Buddhist monks on the next day to the full moon day of Āshādha when they take up abode for the next four months of the rainy season
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • In ancient Indian Buddhist monasteries, the Pavarana ceremony was a day for monks to confess their offenses committed during the rainy season.
  • Pavarana is a Buddhist holy day celebrated on the full moon of the 11th month. It marks the end of the month of Vassa, also known as “Buddhist Lent”. On this day, each monk must come before the community of monks and atone for any offenses committed during the Vassa.
  • During the rainy season, monks who are usually mendicant wanderers gather in monasteries for study and religious discourse.

question: Emperor Harsha's sothward march was stopped on the Narmada river by........... (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: Pulakesin I
option_2: Pulakesin II
option_3: Vikramaditya I
option_4: Vikramaditya II
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Emperor Harsha’s southward march was stopped on the Narmada river by Pulakesin II, a Chalukya king from northern Mysore.
  • Pulakesin II was a powerful ruler from the 7th century who successfully defended his kingdom against Harsha’s invasion.
  • Emperor Harsha was a powerful ruler in North India during the 7th century. He tried to expand his empire to the south, but his march was stopped at the Narmada river.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2003)

1. The last Mauryan ruler, Brihadratha was assassinated by his commander in chief Pushyamitra Sunga

2. The Last Sunga King,Devabhuti was assassinated by his Brahmana minister Vasudeva Kanva who usurped the throne

3. The last ruler of the Kanva dynasty was deposed by the Andhras

Whitch of this statement is/are correct?
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 0nly 2
option_3: Only 3
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The correct answer is (d) 1, 2 and 3, as all three statements are historically accurate. The Mauryan Empire came to an end when its last ruler, Brihadratha Maurya, was assassinated by his own commander-in-chief Pushyamitra Sunga around 185 BCE during a military parade. Pushyamitra then established the Sunga dynasty, marking the transition from the Mauryan to the Sunga rule. 
  • Later, the Sunga line itself ended when the last Sunga ruler, Devabhuti, was murdered by his Brahmana minister Vasudeva Kanva around 73 BCE, who then founded the Kanva dynasty in Magadha. 
  • The Kanvas, however, ruled for a short period and were eventually overthrown by the Andhras (Satavahanas) around 28 BCE, who extended their power into northern India. 
  • Thus, each statement correctly represents the historical sequence of dynastic changes — the fall of the Mauryas, Sungas, and Kanvas — showing how internal conspiracies and assassinations led to successive transfers of power in ancient Magadha.

question:
Consider the following statement: (UPSC Prelims 2003)

1. Vardhamana Mahavira's mother was the daughter of Lichchavi chief Chetaka

2. Guatama Budha’s mother was princess from the Koshalan Dynasty

3. Parshvanatha the twenty third Tirthankara belonged to Banaras

Which of these statements is/are correct?
option_1: Only 1
option_2: 0nly 2
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Vardhamana Mahavira’s mother, Trishala, was the sister of Lichchhavi chief Chetaka.
  • Vardhamana Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism, was born in 599 BCE at Kundagrama near Vaishali. His father was Siddhartha, the head of the Jnatrika clan.
  • Gautama Buddha’s mother, Queen Maya, was from the Koliyan dynasty, not the Kosha-lan dynasty. The Koliyan and Shakya dynasties ruled in Nepal. Queen Maya was married to the Shakya dynasty king Shuddhodana.
  • Gautama Buddha, also known as Siddhartha, was born around 563/480 BCE into a royal family in Nepal. He is the founder of Buddhism.
  • Parshvanatha, the twenty - third Tirthankara, is associated with Banaras.

question:
Consider The Following Statment: (UPSC Prelims 2004)

1. The Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien attended the fourth great Buddhist Council held by Kanishka.

2. The Chinese Pilgrim Hiuen Tsang met Harsha and found him to be antagonistic to Buddhism

Which of the statement given above is/are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Niether 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • There’s no evidence that the Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien attended the fourth Great Buddhist Council held by Kanishka.
  • Fa-Hien visited India in the 5th century CE and recorded his observations in his travel accounts.
  • The fourth Buddhist Council was held in Kashmir during the reign of Kanishka, under the presidency of Vasumitra and Ashvaghosh. Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of Chandra Gupta-II.
  • Buddhist councils are gatherings of Buddhist monastic communities that periodically convene to settle disputes and revise the Buddhist canons.
  • The fourth Buddhist Council was held in the first century AD. The main goal of thisgathering was to recite all of the Gautama Buddha’s teachings and analyze them to see if any had been changed, misinterpreted, or omitted.
  • Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang) was impressed by King Harsha’s support for scholarship and patronage of Buddhism. Hiuen Tsang also stated that he met many rulers who were indifferent or hostile to Buddhism, but King Harsha was an exception.
  • Harsha was a worshiper of Shiva who later became a Mahayana Buddhist. He was tolerant of other faiths and arranged a great assembly at Kanyakubja to popularize and propagate the doctrines of Mahayana Buddhism. Hiuen Tsang presided over this assembly.
  • Harsha was one of the most prominent Indian emperors in the 7th century AD. He built a large empire that extended from north and northwestern India to the Narmada in the south.

question: Which one of the following four Vedas contains an account of magical charms and spells? (UPSC Prelims 2004)
option_1: Rig-Veda
option_2: Yajur-Veda
option_3: Atharva Veda
option_4: Sama-Veda
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Atharva Veda contains magical charms and spells. It is also known as the “veda of magical formulas”.
  • The Atharva Veda is a collection of hymns, prayers, charms, and spells from the Vedic era. It is divided into 20 books, and books 1–7 contain magical prayers for specific purposes.

These include:

  • Spells for long life, Cures, Curses, Love charms, Prayers for prosperity, Charms for kingship and Brahmanhood, Expiations for evil actions.
  • The Atharva Veda also contains charms against possession by demons of disease, and charms for driving away demons. Italso contains charms meant to protect a person against malicious adversaries, such as sorcerers, and “enemies thwarting holy work”.
  • The Atharva Veda is an ancient Hindu scripture written in Vedic Sanskrit around 1200–900 BCE. It is a collection of 20 books, with a total of 730 hymns of about 6,000 stanzas.

Here are some other Vedas:

1. Rig Veda: Rituals for the yajnas

2. Sama Veda: Praise of gods

3. Yajur Veda: Charms and spells to ward off evil spirits

question: With reference to ancient Jainism, Which one of the following statement is correct? (UPSC Prelims 2004)
option_1: Jainism was spread in South India under the leadership of Sthalabahu
option_2: The Jainas who remained under the leadership of Bhadrabahu were called Shvetambaras after the council held at Pataliputra
option_3: Jainism enjoyed the patronage of the Kalinga king Kharavela in the first century BC
option_4: In the initial stage, the Jainas worshipped images unlike the Buddhists; Kharavela is believed to be a follower of Jainism
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Jainism spread in South India under the leadership of Bhadrabahu, not Sthulabahu.
  • Bhadrabahu was a Jain monk and scholar who lived in the court of Chandragupta Maurya. He is believed to have migrated to South India around 300 BCE with a group of Jain monks.
  • A 12-year famine in Magadha led to the spread of Jainism in South India. Many Jains went to South India under Bhadrabahu’s leadership to protect themselves.
  • Bhadrabahu is credited with spreading Jainism in Karnataka, especially in the court of Chandragupta Maurya.
  • Chandragupta Maurya became a Jain ascetic and spent the last years of his life in Karnataka.The Jainas who remained under the leadership of Bhadrabahu were called Shvetambaras after the council held at Pataliputra. •

Jainism has two main variants:

1. Digambar (the naked) and

2. Shvetambara (wearers of white cloths).

  • The monks and nuns of the Shvetambara sect wear simple white garments.
  • This is in contrast to the practice followed by the parallel sect, the Digambara (“Sky clad”), which does not.
  • The Jainas who went to south during the great famine began to be called as digambaras. And those who stayed back in Magadha began to be called as shvetambaras.
  • Jainism enjoyed the patronage of the Kalinga king Kharavela in the first century BC.
  • Kharavela was a king of the Mahameghavahana dynasty of Kalinga, which is now Odisha.
  • He reigned in the second century BC and became a patron of Jainism from the start of his rule. Kharavela is known for supporting various religious orders, including Jainism.
  • Jainism spread to Kalinga in Orissa in the fourth century BC. In the second and first centuries BC, it also reached the southern districts of Tamil Nadu.

question: Who among the following was not a contemporary of the other three........... (UPSC Prelims 2005)
option_1: Bimbisara
option_2: Gautama Buddha
option_3: Milinda
option_4: Prasenjit
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Milinda was not a contemporary of Bimbisara, Gautama Buddha, and Prasenjit.

Here’s some related information about these historical figures:

Bimbisara :

  • A king of Magadha who lived during the 6th and early 5th centuries BCE.

Gautama Buddha :

  • The founder of Buddhism who lived during the 6th and early 5th centuries BCE.

Prasenjit :

  • A king of the Kosala kingdom who lived during the 6th and early 5th centuries BCE.

Milinda :

  • Also known as Menander I, he was a ruler of the Indo-Greek Kingdom who lived during the 2nd century BCE.

question: With reference to the invaders in ancient India, which one of the following is the correct chronological order? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Greeks - Sakas - Kushans
option_2: Greeks -Kushans - Sakas
option_3: Sakas - Greeks - Kushans
option_4: Sakas - Kushans - Greeks
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Greeks, Sakas, and Kushans were all invaders of ancient India. The Greeks were the first to invade, followed by the Sakas, and then the Kushans.
  • The Greeks began invading India in 327 326 BCE, starting with Alexander the Great. The Indo-Greek dynasty ruled from 180–55 BCE.
  • The Sakas, also known as the Indo-Scythians, conquered northwest India in the first century BCE.
  • The Sakas were a nomadic tribe from central Asia who were driven from their homes by the Yueh-Chi tribe around 165 BCE.
  • The Indo-Greeks, Sakas, and Kushans all influenced ancient India’s society, religion, art, literature, and governance.

question: Who among the following laid the foundation of Rashtrakuta empire............. (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Amoghavarsha I
option_2: Dantidurga
option_3: Dhruva
option_4: Krishna I
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Rashtrakuta Empire was one of the most powerful dynasties of early medieval India, and its foundation was laid by Dantidurga (735–756 CE). He established Rashtrakuta power in the Deccan by overthrowing his overlord, the Chalukya king Kirtivarman II, marking the end of Chalukya supremacy in the region.
  • Dantidurga’s military successes, especially against the Chalukyas and the Gurjaras, helped consolidate his position and make the Rashtrakutas an independent ruling power. The dynasty’s capital was later established at Manyakheta (modern Malkhed in Karnataka).
  • Amoghavarsha I was not the founder but one of the most celebrated rulers of the Rashtrakuta dynasty, known for his long and peaceful reign (c. 814–878 CE). He promoted literature, religion, and learning and authored Kavirajamarga, the earliest known work in Kannada literature.
  • Dhruva (r. c. 780–793 CE) was another important ruler who expanded the empire northward and strengthened Rashtrakuta political influence across India, but he too came after the dynasty was already well established.
  • Krishna I (r. c. 756–773 CE), the successor of Dantidurga, was famous for his architectural achievement — the construction of the Kailasa Temple at Ellora — but he did not found the empire.

Therefore, the correct answer is (b)  Dantidurga.

question:
Consider the following statement: (UPSC Prelims 2006)

1. The Ikshvaku rulers of Southern India were antagonistic towards Buddhism

2. The Pala Rulers of Eastern india were patrons of Buddhism

Which of the statement given above is/are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The Ikshvaku dynasty :

  • which ruled parts of Andhra Pradesh in southern India around the 3rd to 4th centuries CE with their capital at Vijayapuri (Nagarjunakonda), were actually known for their support of Buddhism, not hostility toward it.
  • Archaeological evidence from Nagarjunakonda shows that several Buddhist stupas and monasteries were constructed under the patronage of the Ikshvakus.
  • Although they were personally devoted to Brahmanism, they remained tolerant and extended royal patronage to Buddhist institutions and monks, especially those of the Mahasanghika school. Hence, the statement claiming that they were “antagonistic” toward Buddhism is historically inaccurate.

The Pala dynasty (8th–12th century CE) :

  • which ruled Bengal and Bihar, were great supporters of Mahayana and Vajrayana Buddhism. Kings like Dharmapala and Devapala founded and supported several major Buddhist monasteries and universities, such as Vikramashila, Nalanda, and Somapura Mahavihara.
  • The Palas also maintained cultural and religious contacts with Buddhist regions such as Tibet and Southeast Asia, helping to sustain Buddhism long after its decline in other parts of India.

Therefore, since only the second statement is true, the correct answer is (b) 2 only.

question: Mahamastakabhisheka, a great religious event is associated with and done for whom of the following.......... (UPSC Prelims 2009)
option_1: Bahubali
option_2: Buddha
option_3: Mahavir
option_4: Nataraja
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Mahamastakabhisheka is a Jain festival that involves the anointing of a statue of Lord Bahubali with sacred liquids. The ceremony is held every 12 years and is a central part of the Jain tradition.
  • The ceremony is associated with Lord Bahubali, a revered figure in Jainism who gave up his princely status to pursue spiritual enlightenment. The statue is a symbol of peace, non-violence, and the pursuit of spiritual liberation.
  •  The word “Mahamastakabhisheka” is a combination of three words:

1. “Maha” (great)

2. “Masthaka” (head) and

3. “Abhisheka” (anointing)

  • The ceremony is called Maha mastakabhisheka, not Masthakabhisheka, unlike other idols.
  • The ceremony is held in Shravanabelagola, Karnataka, India, and involves the 17.4-meter high monolithic statue of the Siddha Bahubali. The last ceremony was in February 2018, and the next will be in 2030.

question: Anekantavada is a core theory and philosophy of which one of the following? (UPSC Prelims 2009)
option_1: Buddhism
option_2: Janism
option_3: Sikhism
option_4: Vaishnavism
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Anekantavada is a core theory and philosophy of Jainism.
  • Anekantavada is a Jain doctrine that originated in ancient India.
  • It states that reality and truth are complex and have multiple aspects. The doctrine advocates for the principles of multiplicity and relativity of truth. This means that it accepts multiple perspectives as valid, rather than a single indisputable truth.
  • Anekantavada originated from the teachings of Mahāvīra, the 24th Jain Tīrthankara. The details of the doctrine emerged in the 1st millennium CE, from debates between scholars of Jain, Buddhist, and Vedic schools of philosophies.
  • Buddhism is a religion and philosophical tradition that originated in the eastern Gangetic plain in the 5th century BCE. It is also known as Bauddha Dharma and Dharmavinaya.

Jainism :

  • It is also known as Jain Dharma, is an Indian religion that originated in the 6th century BC. Jains believe that reality is made up of two eternal principles:

1. jiva (living) and

2. ajiva (non-living).

  • Jains believe that all things, including plants, animals, and some nonliving things, have souls.

Jainism’s teachings include:

1. Right faith

2. Right knowledge

3. Right conduct, which includes five vows:

i. Ahimsa (non-violence)

ii. Satya (truth)

iii. Asteya (no stealing)

iv. Aparigraha (no acquiring property)

v. Brahmacharya (abstinence)

  • Jains practice ahimsa to avoid bad karma. Ahimsa includes doing no harm to humans, plants, animals, and nature. Jains believe that plants, animals, and some nonliving things have souls, just as humans do.
  • Jainism’s spiritual ideas and history are traced through the succession of 24 tirthankaras. A tirthankara is a saviour and supreme spiritual teacher of the dharma (righteous path). The word tirthankara signifies the founder of a tirtha, which is a fordable passage across the sea of interminable births and deaths, the samsāra. The 24th and last Tirthankara is Lord Mahavira, who was born in the early 6th century BCE into a royal Jain family of ancient India.

Sikhism :

  • It is a monotheistic religion and philosophy that originated in the Punjab region of India in the late 15th century. It is the world’s fifth most popular religion and has over 25 million followers worldwide.
  • Sikhism’s core message is to practice spiritual devotion and reverence of God while also practicing honesty, compassion, humility, and generosity in everyday life. Sikhs believe in equality, service to others, and tolerance for other religions. They also believe that doing good deeds is important for being with God after death.
  • Sikhs are an ethnoreligious group that are neither Hindus nor Muslims. The term Sikh comes from the Sanskrit word śisya, which means “seeker,” “disciple,” or “student”. Sikhs do not have a specific day of worship, but Sunday is a popular day for services.
  • Sikh scriptures are written in the Gurumukhi script, which is unique to Sikhs.

Vaishnavism :

  • It is a major Hindu denomination and a modern form of Hinduism. It is also known as Vishnuism, Vaisnava, or Vaisnavism.
  • Vaishnavism is a tradition that worships Vishnu as the supreme god and his incarnations. Vaishnavas believe that God is the Supreme person, or Krishna, and that the ultimate reality is personal. They believe that by chanting God’s names, the soul can reawaken its original spiritual knowledge.
  • Vaishnavism is founded around 500 BCE and is one of the major Hindu denominations along with Shaivism, Shaktism, and Smartism.

The three main symbols of Vaishnavism are:

1. Shankha: Conch

2. Chakra: Wheel of power

3. Vishnu-padam: Feet of Vishnu Vaishnava literature emerged in Sanskrit and vernacular writings from the 10th through the 16th century.

Some main writings include:

  •  Mahabharata
  •  Ramayana
  •  Bhagavad-gita
  •  Bhagavat Purana
  •  Vishnu Purana
  •  Hymns of the Alvars (Tamil poet-mys tics)
  •  Vedanta Sutras

question:
Regarding the Indus valley civilization, consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2011)

1. It was predominantly a secular civilization and the religious element, though present, did not dominate the scene.

2. During this period, cotton was used for manufacturing textiles in India.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Indus Valley Civilization (IVC) was predominantly secular, with religious ceremonies taking place in homes, small temples, or in the open air. There is no evidence of religious dominance in any discovered settlement.
  • The IVC was a Bronze Age civilization in the northwestern regions of South Asia, lasting from 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE. It is believed that the Harappan religious practices influenced later religions that developed in the Indian subcontinent, such as Jainism, Buddhism, and Hinduism.
  • Discovery has shown that worshipping trees and snakes was considered sacred. The worshipping of the Peepal tree (Sacred fig) was widespread during this period.
  • The use of cotton in textiles may have originated in India in the fifth millennium BC. The Indian subcontinent was a major cotton textile manufacturer before 1800, supplying most of the continent’s market.
  • India held a global monopoly on the manufacturing of cotton textiles for about 3,000 years, from 1500 BC to 1500 AD. During the middle ages, India exported cotton textiles to Eastern and European markets.
  • The earliest surviving Indian cotton threads date to around 4000 BC and dyed fabrics from the region are documented as far back as 2500 BC.
  • According to Marshall, the use of cotton for textiles was exclusively restricted to India during the Indus Valley Civilization period. In ancient Greece and Babylon, the name “India” was shorthand for “cotton”.

Indus Valley Civilization :

  • It was a Bronze Age civilization that lasted from 3300–1300 BCE. It was the largest of the four ancient civilizations, which also included Egypt, Mesopotamia, and China.
  • The IVC was one of the world’s first settled or “urban” civilizations. It extended from modern-day northeast Afghanistan to Pakistan and northwest India.

 The IVC had four main settlements:

1. Mohenjo-Daro and

2. Harappa in Pakistan, and

3. Lothal and

4. Kalibangan in western India.

The IVC made many technological advances, including:

1. Standardized weights and measures

2. Seal carving

3. Metallurgy with copper, bronze, lead, and tin

4. Fire-baked bricks

  • The IVC declined around 1800 BCE due to climate change and migration. Physical evidence suggests climate change which caused flooding, drought, and famine.

Secular Civilization :

  • The Indus Valley Civilization (IVC) is considered to be predominantly secular, with religious ceremonies taking place in homes, small temples, or in the open air. The religious element did not dominate the socio-economic conditions.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization is also known as the Harappan Civilization. The people of the Indus Valley Civilization worshipped Pashupati or the Proto Shiva as their chief deity.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization is believed to have influenced later religions that developed in the Indian subcontinent, such as Jainism, Buddhism, and Hinduism.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization was also predominantly matriarchal, with strong family organizations among the people. Social amusements included hunting wild animals, bullfighting, fishing, and clay modeling.

Textile Manufacturing :

  • The Indus Valley Civilization, which f lourished around 3300 BCE, was one of the earliest influences on Indian textiles. Archaeological evidence suggests that the Indus Valley people were skilled inspinning, weaving, and dyeing cotton, silk, and woolen fabrics.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization is also credited with being the first to spin and weave cotton. In 1929, archaeologists recovered fragments of cotton textiles at Mohenjo Daro, in what is now Pakistan, dating to between 3250 and 2750 BCE. Cottonseeds found at nearby Mehrgarh have been dated to 5000 BCE.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization also exported cotton textiles.
  • Textiles of Indian origin were one of the major products of trade and business in those times.

question:
With reference to the difference between the culture of Rigvedic Aryans and Indus Valley people, which of the following statements is/are correct?  (UPSC Prelims 2011)

1. Rigvedic Aryans used the coat of mail and helmet in warfare whereas the people of the Indus Valley Civilization did not leave any evidence of using them.

2. Rigvedic Aryans knew gold, silver and copper whereas Indus Valley people knew only copper and iron.

3. Rigvedic Aryans had domesticated the horse whereas there is no evidence of Indus Valley people having been aware of this animal.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Rigvedic Aryans used helmets and coats of mail in warfare, while the people of the Indus Valley Civilization did not leave any evidence of using them.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization existed from 3300–1300 BCE and its mature period was from 2600–1900 BCE. The civilization extended along the Indus River from whatis now northeast Afghanistan, into Pakistan and northwest India.
  • The Rigvedic Aryans knew gold, silver, copper, and iron, while the Indus Valley people knew gold, copper, bronze, and tin.
  • The Indus Valley civilization was a Bronze Age civilization, so they were unaware of iron. Iron was discovered in the post-Vedic period.
  • The Rigvedic period, or Early Vedic period, was from 1500–1000 BC. The Vedic Aryans were the authors of the Vedic hymns.
  • The statement that Rigvedic Aryans domesticated horses while there is no evidence that the Indus Valley people were aware of them is generally correct. However, some recent studies suggest that the Indus Valley people may have been aware of horses. The Rigvedic Aryans used horses for daily life and warfare.
  • The earliest evidence of horses being used for transportation comes from chariot burials around 2000 BC. However, there is increasing evidence that horses were domesticated in the Eurasian Steppes around 3500 BC.
  • The Indus Valley people may not have domesticated horses because wild horses in the Indian subcontinent had already gone extinct by the time their civilization rose to prominence.

question: The Jain philosophy holds that the world is created and maintained by..........  (UPSC Prelims 2011)
option_1: Universal Law
option_2: Universal Truth
option_3: Universal Faith
option_4: Universal Soul
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Jain philosophy holds that the world is created and maintained by Universal Law, also known as Dharma. Jainism does not support belief in a creator deity. According to the Jain doctrine, the universe and its constituents have always existed.
  • According to Jain cosmology, the universe functions through the interaction of Jiva and Ajiva

The Jains distinguish two types of space:

1. World space: A vast but limited area where all souls live in the different body-forms they take according to their rebirths in the various worlds.

2. Non-world space: Endless space.

question: India maintained its early cultural contacts and trade links with Southeast Asia across the Bay of Bengal. For this pre-eminence of the early maritime history of the Bay of Bengal, which of the following could be the most convincing Solution/Solutions?  (UPSC Prelims 2011)
option_1: As compared to other countries, India had better ship-building technology in ancient and medieval times.
option_2: The rulers of southern India always patronised traders, Brahmin priests and Buddhist monks in this context
option_3: Monsoon winds across the Bay of Bengal facilitated sea voyages.
option_4: Both (a) and (b) are convincing solutions in this context.
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • India’s ship-building technology was highly advanced during ancient and medieval times. The country had great expertise in constructing and navigating ships, which allowed them to sail across the Bay of Bengal.
  • The Indians were skilled in building sturdy and efficient vessels that could withstand the challenges of long sea voyages.
  • The use of superior ship-building technology gave India an advantage in maintaining cultural contacts and trade links with Southeast Asia.
  • The rules of southern India always patronized traders, brahmin priests and Buddhist monks in this context.
  • Kanishka patronized Buddhist monks, Kushans patronized traders, Cholas patronized brahmin priests.

question:
With reference to the scientific progress of Ancient India, Which of the statements given below are correct?  (UPSC Prelims 2012)

1. Different kinds of specialised surgical instruments were in common use by the 1st century AD.

2. Transplant of internal organs in the human body began in the 3rd century AD.

3. The concept of sine of an angle was known in the 5th century AD.

4. The concept of cyclic quadrilateral was known in the 7th century AD.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 3 and 4 only
option_3: 1, 3 and 4 only
option_4: 1, 2, 3 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Some types of surgical instruments include:

  •  Graspers, such as forceps
  •  Clamps
  •  Surgical scissors
  •  Bone cutters
  •  Needles and sutures
  •  Needle drivers
  • Susruta is considered the first surgeon in the world to describe different types of surgical instruments, including endoscopes. He also documented plastic surgery, including the use of nose skin flaps to replace other parts.
  • There is no evidence that internal organ transplants were performed in India by the beginning of the 3rd century AD.
  • The history of organ transplants began in the 1950s. The first successful kidney transplant in a human was performed in 1954.
  • The first known successful transplants were performed in the 16th century. In 1905, Eduard Zirm performed the first successful transplant between two individuals, transplanting the corneas of an 11-year-old boy into a Czech day laborer.
  • The concept of sine of an angle was known in the 5th century AD.
  • The Indian mathematician and astronomer Aryabhata (476-550) introduced the concept of sine and explored its properties in his work Aryabhatiya, written in 499.
  • Aryabhata’s work contains trigonometric tables and formulas, and includes tables of half chords that are 120 times the sine.
  • The word “sine” comes from the Latin word sinus, which means “bay” or “inlet”.
  • The concept of cyclic quadrilaterals was f irst discussed in the 7th century AD by the Indian mathematician Bhaskara in his work Lilavati.
  • A cyclic quadrilateral is a quadrilateral that can be inscribed in a circle.
  • Brahmagupta’s formula is used to find the area of a cyclic quadrilateral given the lengths of its sides. The formula is named after the 7th century Indian mathematician Brahmagupta.

question:
With reference to the Guilds (Shrenis) of ancient India that played a very important role in the country’s economy, which of the following statements is/are true? (UPSC Prelims 2012)

  1. Every Guild was registered with the central authority of the state and the King was the chief administrative authority on them.
  2. The wages, rules of work, standards and prices were fixed by the Guild.
  3. The guild had judicial powers over its own members.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 3 only
option_3: 2 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • In ancient India, a shreni was a group of merchants, artisans, and traders. The word shreni is Sanskrit for “śreni” or “śhrenī” and Prakrit for “seni”.
  • Shrenis provided training, distributed finished products, and procured raw materials. They also organized trade and served as banks.
  • Each shreni was typically for a specific group of people who were involved in the same activity or vocation. The head of a shreni was called a Shrestha or Shreshti, which means “His Excellency”.
  • Shrenis also provided welfare benefits for their members. Some shrenis handled disputes between husbands and wives.
  • Others supported their members during illnesses and cared for their widows and children after their deaths. Guilds were autonomous bodies that were not directly controlled by the king or the state.
  • Guilds were associations of merchants and artisans who oversaw the practice of their trade in a particular territory.
  • The earliest guilds were organizations of tradespeople belonging to a professional association.
  • Guilds fixed wages, rules of work, standards, and prices. They also had judicial powers over their own members.
  • Guilds (shrenis) of ancient India had judicial powers over their members. Guilds had their own judicial systems to resolve disputes between members. They could impose fines and expel members who broke the guild’s rules and regulations.
  • Guilds could also try members for offenses based on their own customs and practices, which were almost considered law. Guilds could even arbitrate between members and their wives.
  • Guilds were professional bodies of people who came together to control quality production, create a sound business ethic, and maintain fair wages and prices.
  • Guilds also determined prices, determined the mode of sale, maintained the quality ofproducts, divided labor, framed labor laws, and determined working hours.
  • Guilds were registered with the central authority of the state, and the king was the chief administrative authority on them. The earliest evidence of trade guilds in India is from the 5th century BC.

question: The Religion of early Vedic Aryans was primarily of........... (UPSC Prelims 2012)
option_1: Bhakti
option_2: Image worship and yajnas
option_3: Worship of nature and yajnas
option_4: Worship of nature and bhakti
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The religion of the early Vedic Aryans was primarily of worship of nature and Yajnas.
  • The Vedic Aryans were a religious people who believed in a simple religion. They worshipped natural forces like the sun, fire, wind, dawn, water, rain, and earth. They also worshipped other manifestations of nature like the moon, sky, thunder, and air. The Vedic Aryans composed hymns in praise of nature.
  • The Vedic Age was between 1500 BC and 600 BC. The early Vedic Aryan tribe was called the Indo-Aryans.
  • Yajna is a Vedic tradition that involves performing rituals in front of a sacred fire. The tradition has evolved from offering oblations and libations into sacred fire to symbolic offerings in the presence of sacred fire.

question:
With reference to the history of Ancient India, which of the following was/were common to both Buddhism and Jainism? (UPSC Prelims 2012)

1. Avoidance of extremities of penance and enjoyment

2. Indifference to the authority of Vedas

3. Denial of the efficacy of rituals

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Buddhism and Jainism share many similarities, including:

1. Karma :

  • Both religions believe in karma, which is the attachment of positive and negative forces to the soul.

2. Salvation :

  • Both religions believe that salvation can only be achieved through nirvana.

3. Helping others :

  • Both religions believe in helping others and teaching their values to others.

4. Materialistic world :

  • Both religions teach to stay away from the materialistic world and desires.

5. Moral lives :

  • Both religions give importance to the moral lives of people.

6. Harm to humans :

  • The teachings of Jainism and Buddhism condemn all sorts of harm meted to humans or living beings.

7. Non-violence :

  • Both religions believe and propagate non violence, good social conduct, charity, and generosity.

8. Atheism :

  • Both religions are atheistic in the sense that they don’t believe in there’s a creator god or any super being.

9. Common background :

  • Both religions have a common background associated with the Aryan Culture.

10. Against Vedas :

  • Both religions were against Vedas and the Vedic culture as well as against Brahmanism.

11. Denial of efficiency of rituals :

  • Both religions deny the efficiency of rituals.

12. Only those who left their homes could gain true knowledge :

  • Both religions believe that only those who left their homes could gain true knowledge.

13. Non injury to animal life :

Buddhism and Jainism also share some other similarities, including:

1. Both religions emerged as a new religious ideas from late 6th century BCE.

2. Both religions preached Satya, Ahimsa, Brahmacharya, and detachment from the materialistic world.

question:
Which of the following characterises/ characterises the people of the Indus Civilization? (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. They possessed great palaces and temples.

2. They worshipped both male and female deities.

3. They employed horse-drawn chariots in warfare.

Select the correct statement/statements using the codes given below.
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: 1, 2 and 3
option_4: None of the statements given above is correct
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Indus Valley Civilization (2600–1900 BCE) was one of the world’s earliest urban civilizations, known for its planned cities, advanced drainage systems, and standardized weights and measures.
  • However, there is no archaeological evidence of great palaces or temples (Statement 1), unlike in contemporary civilizations such as Egypt or Mesopotamia.
  • The structures discovered at Harappa and Mohenjo-daro, like the Great Bath and granaries, were likely public or administrative buildings, not royal or religious palaces. This indicates that political or religious authority was not concentrated in a single ruler or temple-based institution.
  • Statement 2 is correct, as excavations have revealed evidence suggesting that the Harappans worshipped both male and female deities.
  • The discovery of numerous terracotta female figurines, believed to represent a Mother Goddess, indicates fertility worship, while the presence of seals depicting a proto-Shiva figure (often associated with Pashupati, the “Lord of Animals”) suggests male deity worship. This shows a balanced religious belief system centered on fertility, nature, and creation.
  • Statement 3 is incorrect, since there is no evidence of horse-drawn chariots or the domestication of horses during the mature Harappan phase. Horses appear in India much later, during the Vedic period. The Harappans used bullock carts and other simple vehicles for transport, not horses or chariots for warfare.

Therefore, among the three statements, only Statement 2 correctly characterizes the people of the Indus Civilization — making option (b) the right answer.

question:
The Chinese traveller Yuan Chwang (Hiuen Tsang) who visited India recorded the general conditions and culture of India at that time. In this context, which of the following statements is/are correct? (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. The roads and river routes were completely immune from robbery.

2. As regards punishment for offences, ordeals by fire, water and poison were the instruments for determining the innocence or guilt of a person.

3. The tradesmen had to pay duties at ferries and barrier stations.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Chinese traveler Yuan Chwang (Hiuen Tsang) recorded instances of robbery on roads and river routes in his travelogue. He also mentioned that merchants had to pay a tax to the local authorities for protection against robbers.
  • Yuan Chwang also recorded that tradesmen had to pay duties at ferries and barrier stations. He praised the emperor Harsha and his administration, but also wrote that the roads were not safe and he himself got robbed more than once.
  • As regards punishment for offences, ordeals by fire, water and poison were the instruments for determining the innocence or guilt of a person.
  • Yuan Chwang mentions that ordeals by fire, water and poison were used as a means of determining the guilt or innocence of a person.
  • Yuan Chwang was a Chinese Buddhist monk who traveled to India in 630 AD to study Buddhism. He visited the Pallava capital Kanchi during the reign of Narasimhavarman I.
  • Yuan Chwang’s records also indicate the presence of taxation and trade regulations. He also recorded that the government was liberal in tax and revenue collection.

question: Who of the following had first deciphered the edicts of Emperor Ashoka? (UPSC Prelims 2016)
option_1: Georg Buhler
option_2: James Prinsep
option_3: Max Muller
option_4: William Jones
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • James Prinsep was the first person to decipher the edicts of Emperor Ashoka, around 1837.
  • Prinsep was a British archaeologist and historian who held a high rank in the British East India Company.
  • He converted the edicts into normal language, and his decoding of the Brahmi script inspired other historians to decode other parts of Indian political history.

The Edicts of Ashoka :

  • These are a valuable historical source that provide insights into the governance and moral philosophy of one of ancient India’s most renowned rulers.
  • Ashoka, who ruled India from 269 BCE to 232 BCE, is known for his extensive use of edicts to communicate with his subjects. He had edicts inscribed on pillars, rocks, and caves throughout his empire, which covered most of the Indian subcontinent.

Some of the Edicts of Ashoka include:

Minor Rock Edicts :

  • The earliest inscriptions from Ashoka, these edicts give Ashoka the title “Devanampriya,” which means “Beloved-of-the-Gods”. They also promote the teachings of the Buddha and the moral laws of the Dhamma.

Kalinga Rock Edicts :

  • This rock edict constructed by Ashoka specifies the Dharma or the central law of Buddha.

Pillar edicts of Ashoka :

  • These are a number of columns inscribed with edicts by the Mauryan king, Ashoka, during his reign in the mid-3rd century BC.

Sarnath Edict :

  • This edict was issued by Ashoka at Sarnath, Uttar Pradesh. In this edict, Ashoka states that he has appointed officials to oversee the sangha and to prevent disputes and divisions.

Kandahar Greek Edicts :

  • These are among the Major Rock Edicts, written in the Greek language and Prakrit language.

question: With reference to the cultural history of India, the memorising of chronicles, dynastic histories and epic tales was the profession of which of the following? (UPSC Prelims 2016)
option_1: Shramana
option_2: Parivraajaka
option_3: Agrahaarika
option_4: Magadha
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Magadhas were the people who memorized chronicles, dynastic histories, and epic tales in India’s cultural history. They were storytellers who preserved India’s cultural heritage through oral traditions.
  • The Magadhas and Sutas were descendants of priestly families from the Vedic period. They were singers and narrators who sang praises of their patrons and told stories of their valor.
  • Magadha was a powerful kingdom and empire in ancient India that lasted from 684 BCE to 320 BCE. It was located in what is now west-central Bihar state, in northeastern India.
  • Magadha was one of the sixteen Mahajanapadas, or “Great Kingdoms”, of the Second Urbanization. It was located on the upper and lower parts of the Gangetic valley and on the mainland route between west and east India.
  • Magadha’s success was partly due to the political ambition of its king, Bimbisara (c. 543–491 BCE). Bimbisara conquered Anga, which gave him access to the Ganges delta, a valuable asset for the nascent maritime trade.
  • Magadha was the nucleus of several larger kingdoms or empires between the 6th century bce and the 8th century ce.
  • The Magadha people are mentioned in ancient texts like the Atharvaveda along with other groups.

Profession of Magadha :

  • The Magadha people’s primary occupation was agriculture. They grew a variety of crops, including rice, wheat, barley, and millet, and raised livestock, such as cattle, sheep, and goats.
  • The Greek traveler Megasthenes (c. 350–c. 290 BC) identified seven occupational groups in Magadha, including farmers, shepherds, soldiers, and magistrates.
  • Magadhas also memorized chronicles, dynastic histories, and epic tales, which helped to keep alive the stories of the great kings, heroes, and gods of ancient India. They were also instrumental in spreading the knowledge of Indian culture to distant lands.
  • Magadha was an ancient kingdom located in present-day Bihar and parts of Uttar Pradesh. It was a significant part of ancient India’s history and development. Magadha was known for its political power, cultural achievements, and strategic location.

Shramanas :

  • They were ascetics and renouncers in ancient India who practiced a strict and self-denying lifestyle in pursuit of spiritual liberation. They are also known as monks. The Sramana movement gave rise to Jainism and Buddhism.
  •  The Shramanas included many groups, sects, and varieties of opinions, including: Buddhists, Jains, Materialist Lokayatas, Ajivikas.
  • The Shramanas rejected the authority of the Brahmins and opposed the ritualistic orthodox ideas of the Brahmanical order.
  • They also rejected elaborate rituals because they felt commoners felt left out of them. They also criticized sacrificial worship because of their belief in Ahimsa or non violence.
  • The Shramanas established viharas or monasteries where they meditated. They also built stupas or dome-like monuments where the common folk could visit and connect with the Buddhist religion.

Parivrajakas :

  • They are people who left their homes and wandered from village to village and forestto forest. They are also known as Bhikshus, which means “mendicants” or “those who begged”.
  •  Some famous examples of parivrajakas include: Vardhamana Mahavira, Gauthama Buddha, Makhkhali Goshala, Ajita Keshkambalin.
  • The Parivrajaka dynasty ruled parts of central India during the 5th and 6th centuries. The kings of this dynasty bore the title Maharaja, and probably ruled as feudatories of the Gupta Empire.

Agraharika :

  • It refers to individuals who were given land or villages by rulers to maintain temples. The term is also used to refer to Brahmins who were given land grants by the state in the 5th-6th centuries.
  • These villages were called Agrahaara, Brahmadeya, or Sashana, and the Brahmins were called Agraharika. The Agrahaara villages were revenue free and had some privileges.
  • Agrahaarika is related to land grants given to Brahmins.

question:
In the context of the history of India, consider the following pairs:  (UPSC Prelims 2016)

Term

Description

Eripatti

Land revenue which was set apart for the maintenance of the village tank

Taniyurs

Villages donated to single Brahmin or a group of Brahmins

Ghatikas

Colleges generally attached to the temples

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Which of the pairs given above is correctly matched?
option_1: 1 and 2
option_2: 3 only
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

Eripatti :

  • It is a term for land where the revenue is set aside for the maintenance of the village tank. This land was donated by individuals and is only known in South India.
  • The village tank was a vital source of water for irrigation and other uses, and its maintenance was crucial for the agricultural economy of the village.
  • Eripatti can also refer to a long tract of land, along a canal, reserved for making a mound on.

Taniyurs :

  • They were villages that were donated to a single Brahmin or a group of Brahmins. This practice was common in the early medieval period, especially in the Tamil Nadu region of South India.
  • Wealthy individuals or kings would donate villages to Brahmins as a way of gaining religious merit and ensuring the performance of religious rites.
  • Taniyurs were also a type of administrative unit, where a very large village would be administered as a single unit.
  • During the Chola regime, Taniyurs developed out of Brahmadeyas and temple settlements and could include several hamlets and revenue villages.

Ghatikas :

  • They were educational institutions in ancient India that were associated with temples or royal courts. They were active during the Pallava period and later.
  • Ghatikas offered a wide range of subjects, including astronomy, mathematics, logic, grammar, and religious texts. They were located in temples or near shrines and attracted students from India and abroad.
  • The Ghatika at Kanchipuram was a famous center of education and learning. It attracted students from India and abroad, including Mayurasarman, the founder of the Kadamba dynasty, and Dharmapala, who later became the Head of the Ancient Nalanda University.

question: Which one of the following is not a Harappan site? (UPSC Prelims 2019)
option_1: Chanhudaro
option_2: Kot Diji
option_3: Sohgaura
option_4: Desalpur
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Sohgaura is not a Harappan site. The other three options, Chanhudaro, Kot Diji, and Desalpur, are all Harappan sites.
  • Sohgaura is located in Gorakhpur, Uttar Pradesh, and is known for the Sohgaura copper plate inscription.
  • The inscription is written in Prakrit in the Brahmi script and is believed to be from the Mauryan period.
  • The text of the plate mentions the establishment of two grain depots to fight against famine.
  • Chanhudaro and Desalpur are both located in Pakistan and Gujarat, respectively. Kot Diji is located in Pakistan.

question: In which of the following relief sculpture inscriptions is ‘Ranyo Ashoka’ (King Ashoka) mentioned along with the stone portrait of Ashoka? (UPSC Prelims 2019)
option_1: Kanganahalli
option_2: Sanchi
option_3: Shahbazgarhi
option_4: Sohgaura
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The site of Kanganahalli, located near Sannati in Karnataka, is an important Buddhist archaeological site belonging to the Satavahana period.
  • Excavations at Kanganahalli revealed a unique relief sculpture depicting a portrait of Emperor Ashoka along with an inscription referring to him as “Ranyo Ashoka”, meaning King Ashoka. This discovery is significant because it provides one of the very few sculptural representations of Ashoka and confirms his royal identity through inscriptional evidence.
  • The site contains several beautifully carved panels illustrating events from the Buddha’s life and donors’ inscriptions, offering valuable insight into early Buddhist art and Mauryan legacy in the Deccan.

Sanchi :

  • It is an important Buddhist site associated with Ashoka — who built one of the earliest stupas there — it does not contain any inscription mentioning “Ranyo Ashoka” or his portrait.
  • The sculptures at Sanchi are symbolic and narrative, focusing on Jataka tales and Buddhist symbols rather than depicting specific rulers.

Shahbazgarhi :

  • It is located in present-day Pakistan, is famous for containing one of the Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka, inscribed in Kharosthi script, but it does not have any relief sculpture or portrait of Ashoka himself. It’s a textual inscription site rather than an artistic or sculptural one.

Sohgaura :

  • It is located in Uttar Pradesh, is known for the Sohgaura copper plate inscription, which is one of the earliest known examples of written records in India, possibly pre-Mauryan, relating to famine relief administration. It has no connection to Ashoka’s portrait or the term “Ranyo Ashoka.”

Therefore, among all the options, only Kanganahalli provides both an inscription naming “Ranyo Ashoka” and a stone portrait of the emperor, making option (a) the correct answer.

question: With reference to forced labour (Vishti) in India during the Gupta period, which one of the following statements is correct? (UPSC Prelims 2019)
option_1: It was considered a source of income for the State, a sort of tax paid by the people.
option_2: It was totally absent in the Madhya Pradesh and Kathiawar regions of the Gupta Empire.
option_3: The forced labourer was entitled to weekly wages.
option_4: The eldest son of the labourer was sent as the forced labourer.
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Vishti, or forced labor, was a common practice in ancient India, especially during the Gupta and Mauryan periods. It was a system of compulsory labor where people were forced to work for the state or wealthy individuals without pay.
  • Vishti was a source of income for the state and was considered a type of tax paid by the people. As a result, Vishti laborers were not entitled to weekly wages.
  • Vishti was mentioned alongside taxes in land grant inscriptions, suggesting that it was considered a source of income for the state. The practice was prevalent in all parts of the Gupta Empire, although the intensity and extent may have varied.
  • Forced labor, or unfree labor, is any work relation where people are employed against their will.
  • This can include threats of destitution, detention, or violence, including death or other forms of extreme hardship to either themselves or members of their families

question: With reference to the history of India, the terms “kulyavapa” and “dronavapa” denote........... (UPSC Prelims 2020)
option_1: Measurement of land
option_2: Coins of different monetary value
option_3: Classification of urban land
option_4: Religious rituals
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The terms “kulyavapa” and “dronavapa” refer to land measurement units in ancient India. “Kulyavapa” was used in the western parts of India, while “dronavapa” was used in the northern parts.
  • The term “vap” means “to sow”. The element “vape” in the compound “kulyavapa” means the act of sowing (throwing or scattering).
  • During the Gupta period, the methods of Adhavapa (3/8-1/2 acres), Dronavapa (1/2 2 acres), and Kulyavapa (12-16 acres) were used for measurement.

question: With reference to the period of the Gupta dynasty in ancient India, the towns Ghantasala, Kadura and Chaul were well known as........... (UPSC Prelims 2020)
option_1: Ports handling foreign trade
option_2: Capitals of powerful kingdoms
option_3: Places of exquisite stone art and architecture
option_4: Important Buddhist pilgrimage centres
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • During the Gupta dynasty in ancient India, the towns of Ghantasala, Kadura, and Chaul were known as ports that handled foreign trade. These ports were important centers of commerce and played a crucial role in international trade.
  • The Gupta Empire (320-550 CE) had extensive trade contacts with various regions and peoples, including the Roman Empire, Southeast Asia, Central Asia, and China. The most significant of these trade contacts were with the Roman Empire.

Ghantasala :

  • It was a port town on the Krishna River in the Andhra region during the Gupta period in ancient India. Ghantasala, along with Kadura and Chaul, were important ports that handled foreign trade and helped establish India as a major center of international trade.
  • Ghantasala is now a village in the Krishna district of Andhra Pradesh, about 21 km west of Machilipatnam and 11 km east of the Krishna River.
  • In ancient times, it was known as Katakasila and was a renowned Buddhist center. It is a popular destination for history-lovers and archaeologists, with attractions including a Buddhist stupa and other Buddhist sculptures that were unearthed in 1919-20.

Kadura :

  • It was a port on the west coast of India, near present-day Mumbai, during the Gupta period. It was a major center of trade and commerce, known for exporting textiles, pearls, and precious stones.
  • Kadura and Ghantasala were ports in the Andhra region. Other ports in western India included Kalyan, Chaul, Broach, and Cambay.

Chaul :

  • It is a historic town in the Raigad district of Maharashtra, India. In early historical times, it was an important port on the West Coast of India. In 1357 CE, Chaul was recorded as one of the important towns of the Bahmani Sultanate.
  • In the 16th century, the Portuguese were granted permission to build a fort at Lower Chaul in exchange for a guaranteed supply of 300 horses per year from Persia or Arabia. In 1508, the Portuguese and an Egyptian Mamluk fleet fought a naval battle in the harbor of Chaul, which ended in a Mamluk victory.

question:
Who among the following rulers advised his subjects through this inscription? (UPSC Prelims 2020)

“Whosoever praises his religious sect or blames other sects out of excessive devotion to his own sect, with the view of glorifying his own sect, he rather injures his own sect very severely.”
option_1: Ashoka
option_2: Samudragupta
option_3: Harshavardhana
option_4: Krishnadeva Raya
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The inscription “Whosoever praises his religious sect or blames other sects out of excessive devotion to his own sect, with the view of glorifying his own sect, he rather injures his own sect very severely” is from the 12th Major Rock Edict of Ashoka.
  • Ashoka was an Indian emperor who ruled the Maurya dynasty from 268 to 232 BCE. He is known for his contributions to the spread of Buddhism across ancient India and his policies of non-violence, religious tolerance, and social welfare.
  • Ashoka’s inscriptions can be classified into Pillar Edicts, Major Rock Edicts, and Minor Rock Edicts. The inscription reflects Ashoka’s policy of religious tolerance and his desire to promote harmony amongdifferent religious sects.
  • Ashoka’s Dhamma was a way of life enshrined in a code of behavior and a set of ideals that he advised his subjects to follow to live in peace and prosperity.

Dhamma policies included the following:

1. Ahimsa (non-violence) and

2. truthfulness should be practiced.

question:
Consider the following pairs: (UPSC Prelims 2021)

 

Historical place

Well-known for

1.

Burzahom

Rock -cut shrines

2.

Chandra- ketugarh

Terracotta art

3.

Ganeshwar

Copper artefacts

Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Burzahom site in Srinagar, India is known for its Neolithic habitation and pit dwellings. The site documents the transition of human habitation patterns from the Neolithic period to the Megalithic period to the early Historic period. The site also provides insight into the material equipment of the Neolithic people. The Burzahom site chronicles life in northern India from 3000 BCE to 1000 BCE.

Chandraketugarh :

  • It is a historical and archaeological site in West Bengal, India. It is known for its terracotta art, which is comparable to other better-known sites. The terracotta objects found at Chandraketugarh date back to between the third century BCE and the third century CE.
  • Chandraketugarh is also known for the remains of a fortified ancient city that was occupied from the 4th century BCE to the 12th century CE. The site is named after King Chandraketu, who ruled the kingdom.
  • Ganeshwar is a village in the Sikar District of Rajasthan. It’s known for its hot sulfur springs, which are believed to cure skin ailments. The village is also a popular tourist destination for its historical significance and ruins of a 4,000-year-old civilization.
  • In 1977, excavations at Ganeshwar revealed copper objects, including arrowheads, spearheads, fish hooks, bangles, and chisels. The Ganeshwar culture is attributed to the pre-Harappan period, based on its microliths and other stone tools.

question: Which one of the following ancient towns is well-known for its elaborate system of water harvesting and management by building a series of dams and channelizing water into connected reservoirs? (UPSC Prelims 2021)
option_1: Dholavira
option_2: Kalibangan
option_3: Rakhigarhi
option_4: Ropar
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The ancient city of Dholavira is one of the most remarkable and well-preserved urban settlements in South Asia dating from the 3rd to mid-2nd millennium BCE (Before Common Era).
  • Discovered in 1968, the site is set apart by its unique characteristics, such as its water management system, multi layered defensive mechanisms, extensive use of stone in construction and special burial structures. It has a complex system for collecting and storing rain water within several reservoirs.
  • Planners in the ancient city of Dholavira had conceptualised an amazing system of drains, dams and tanks to manage water.

question:
From the decline of Guptas until the rise of Harshavardhana in the early seventh century, which of the following kingdoms were holding power in Northern India? (UPSC Prelims 2021)

1. The Guptas of Magadha

2. The Paramaras of Malwa

3. The Pushyabhutis of Thanesar

4. The Maukharis of Kanauj

5. The Yadavas of Devagiri

6. The Maitrakas of Valabhi

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
option_1: 1, 2 and 5
option_2: 1, 3, 4 and 6
option_3: 2, 3 and 4
option_4: 5 and 6
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • After the decline of the Guptas, the Later Guptas succeeded as the rulers of Magadha during the early seventh century. They ruled parts of the northern Including Magadha (current day Bihar).
  • The Paramara dynasty ruled the western and central parts of India including Malwa, between the 9th and 14th century AD, before being captured by Delhi Sultanate rulers.
  • The Pushyabhutis of Thanesar (current day Haryana) ruled various parts of Northern India during the 6th and 7th century AD.
  • The Maukharis dynasty ruled over parts of Uttar Pradesh and Magadha including Kannauj. The dynasty ruled till around 606 AD when it was reconquered by the Later Guptas.
  • The Yadavas of Devagiri ruled from around 1187 to 1317 AD in western parts of India. The Maitrakas of Valabhi ruled western and northern parts of current-day Gujarat during the late 5th century to late 8th century AD.

question: With reference to the history of ancient India, Bhavabhuti, Hastimalla and Kshemeshvara were famous........... (UPSC Prelims 2020)
option_1: Jain monks
option_2: Playwrights
option_3: Temple architects
option_4: Philosophers
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Bhavabhuti, Hastimalla, and Kshemeshvara were famous playwrights in ancient India.
  • Bhavabhuti was a Sanskrit playwright and poet who lived in the 8th century. He is believed to have been the court poet of King Yashovarman of Kannauj. His plays include Mahaviracharita, Malatimadhava, and Uttararamacharita.
  • Hastimalla was a Kannada poet and playwright in the Hoysala Empire. He was a Jain poet in the court of the Solanki rulers and wrote eight plays, including Vikrant Kaurava and Subhadra Harana.
  • Kshemeshvara was also a playwright in ancient India.

question:
With reference to the scholars/literatures of ancient India, consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2020)

1. Panini is associated with Pushyamitra Shunga.

2. Amarasimha is associated with Harshavardhana.

3. Kalidasa is associated with Chandra Gupta-II.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • According to some sources, there is no evidence that Panini was associated with Pushyamitra Shunga.
  • Panini was a Sanskrit grammarian from ancient India, known for his work Ashtadhyayi.
  • Ashtadhyayi is one of the earliest known works on linguistics. Panini is also known as the creator of Sanskrit and the father of linguistics.
  • Pushyamitra Shunga was a brahman ruler and fighter who lived around 138 BCE. He was the co-founder of the Shunga empire, which ruled from 185 BCE to 73 BCE.

Amarasimha :

  • It is not associated with Harshavardhana. Amarasimha is a Sanskrit grammarian and poet who lived in the 5th century CE, while Harshavardhana was a ruler who lived in the 7th century CE.
  • Amarasimha is traditionally considered to be one of the nine gems (Navaratnas) at the court of the legendary king Vikramaditya of Ujjain, not Harshavardhana.
  • Amarasimha is also known as a famous lexicographer of ancient India. His renowned work, the Amara-Kosha, is a lexicon of Sanskrit words in three books. It is sometimes called the Trikāndī or the “Tripartite” and contains 10,000 words.

Kalidasa :

  • He is a classical Sanskrit writer and poet who is associated with the court of the Gupta ruler Chandragupta II, also known as Chandragupta Vikramaditya. Kalidasa is considered one of the greatest writers of ancient India, and is known for his plays, including Shakuntala and Meghadutam, as well as his epic and lyrical poetry.
  • Some scholars, including William Jones, A. B. Keith, and Vasudev Vishnu Mirashi, believe that Kalidasa may have lived during the reign of Chandragupta II, which lasted from 380–415 CE. The most popular theory is that Kalidasa lived around the 4th–5th century CE.

question:
According to Kautilya’s Arthashastra, which of the following are correct? (UPSC Prelims 2022)

1. A person could be a slave as a result of a judicial punishment. 

2. If a female slave bore her master a son, she was legally free.

3. If a son born to a female slave was fathered by her master the son was entitled to the legal status of the master’s son.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • According to Kautilya, slavery (dāsa system) in ancient India was a recognized institution but was subject to regulation and legal safeguards. 
  • Statement 1 is correct because a person could indeed become a slave as a result of judicial punishment. Those who committed crimes, defaulted on debts, or were captured in war could be reduced to servitude as part of their penalty, reflecting the socio-legal nature of slavery during that period.
  • Statement 2 is also correct since Kautilya clearly mentions that if a female slave bore her master a son, she became legally free. This provision aimed to ensure that women who bore their master’s children could no longer be treated as property, thereby granting them social recognition and partial emancipation. 
  • Statement 3 is likewise correct because, if a son born to a slave woman was fathered by her master, that child was legally entitled to the status of the master’s son, provided the father acknowledged him. Such children could share inheritance rights and were not treated as slaves, showing a degree of social inclusivity and legal fairness in family matters.

Thus, Kautilya’s Arthashastra, while permitting slavery, introduced several humane provisions to protect the rights of slaves, particularly women and their children, making all three statements—1, 2, and 3—correct.

question:
With reference to Indian history, consider the following texts........... (UPSC Prelims 2022)

1. Nettipakarana

2. Parishishta Parvan

3. Avadanasataka

4. Trishashtilakshana Mahapurana

Which of the above are Jaina texts?
option_1: 1, 2 and 3
option_2: 2 and 4 only
option_3: 1, 3 and 4
option_4: 2, 3 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Parishishtaparvan :

  • Also known as the Sthaviravalicharitra, this 12th-century Sanskrit mahakavya by Hemachandra details the histories of the earliest Jain teachers. The poem is also notable for providing information on the political history of ancient India.

Trishashtilakshana Mahapurana :

  • A major Jain text composed largely by Acharya Jinasena during the rule of Rashtrakuta.

Nettipakarana :

  • A mythological Buddhist scripture related to Buddhism’s Pali Canon. It is sometimes included in the Khuddaka Nikaya of Theravada Buddhism’s Pali Canon.

Avadanashataka :

  • Also known as “A Hundred Glorious Deeds,” this text is a Buddhist text that is an anthology in Sanskrit of one hundred Buddhist legends.

question:
With reference to Indian history, consider the following pairs: (UPSC Prelims 2022)

 

Historical person

Known as

1.

Aryadeva

Jaina scholar

2.

Dignaga

Buddhist scholar

3.

Nathamuni

Vaishnava scholar

 

 

 

 

 

How many pairs given above are correctly matched?
option_1: None of the pairs
option_2: Only one pair
option_3: Only two pairs
option_4: All three pairs
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  •  Pair 1 is incorrect : Āryadeva (3rd century), a disciple of Nāgārjuna, is a central figure in the development of early Indian Madhyamaka philosophy. The Madhyamaka school of Buddhism, the followers of which are called Mādhyamikas, was one of the two principal schools of Mahāyāna Buddhism
  •  Pair 2 is correct : Dignāga, (born c. 480 CE—died c. 540), was a Buddhist logician and author of the Pramasamuccaya (“Compendium of the Means of True Knowledge”), a work that laid the foundations of Buddhist logic.
  •  Pair 3 is correct : Nathamuni founded a Sanskrit-Tamil school at Srirangam (Tamil Nadu state), which continues to be a great Vaishnavite centre in South India.

question: In which one of the following regions was Dhanyakataka, which flourished as prominent Buddhist centre under the Mahasanghikas, located? (UPSC Prelims 2023)
option_1: Andhra
option_2: Gandhara
option_3: Kalinga
option_4: Magadha
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Dhanyakataka :

  • Also known as Dharanikota, was one of the most famous early Buddhist centres in southern India. It was located near Amarāvati on the banks of the Krishna River, in the present-day Guntur District of Andhra Pradesh.
  • The site became a thriving centre of Buddhist art, architecture, and learning between the 3rd century BCE and the 3rd century CE. The region came under the rule of the Satavahana dynasty, who were known for their patronage of Buddhism.

The Amarāvati Stupa :

  • Built at Dhanyakataka, was among the largest and most beautifully decorated stupas in India. It contained intricate relief carvings depicting scenes from the Jātaka tales (stories of the Buddha’s previous lives) and the life of the historical Buddha.
  • The inscriptions and sculptures found here reveal that Dhanyakataka was a stronghold of the Mahāsāṃghika school of Buddhism, one of the early Buddhist sects that emphasized the transcendental nature of the Buddha and the concept of multiple Buddhas and Bodhisattvas. This school later influenced the rise of Mahayana Buddhism.
  • Inscriptions in Prakrit and Brahmi script found at Amarāvati also mention donations by monks, nuns, and lay devotees, showing the wide social base of Buddhism in the Andhra region.
  • Excavations at Dhanyakataka have unearthed relic caskets, coins, railings, and limestone sculptures that confirm its identity as an important Buddhist pilgrimage and monastic centre.

Gandhāra :

  • The Gandhāra region was located in the northwestern part of the Indian subcontinent (modern-day Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan). It was indeed a major Buddhist cultural area but is famous for Gandhāran art, which blended Indian and Greco-Roman styles, especially during the Kushan period (1st–3rd centuries CE). Prominent sites include Taxila, Peshawar, and Shahbazgarhi, but not Dhanyakataka.

Kaliṅga :

  • Kaliṅga corresponds roughly to modern Odisha and parts of northern Andhra Pradesh. After the famous Kalinga War (261 BCE), Emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism and spread it throughout the region.
  • Many Buddhist monasteries and stupas such as those at Udayagiri, Ratnagiri, and Lalitgiri flourished here. However, these were later centres and distinct from Dhanyakataka, which was located further south in the Krishna river valley.

Magadha :

  • Magadha (modern Bihar) was the heartland of early Buddhism, where Gautama Buddha attained enlightenment at Bodh Gaya, preached his first sermon at Sarnath, and where Nalanda and Vikramashila universities later emerged as great Buddhist centres.
  • However, Dhanyakataka was far away in the Deccan and belonged to the southern Buddhist tradition, not the Gangetic one.

Hence the correct region is Andhra.
